are a professional [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") team based in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"). The Eagles compete in the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) as a member of the [National Football Conference](/wiki/National_Football_Conference "National Football Conference") (NFC) [East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") division. The team plays its home games at [Lincoln Financial Field](/wiki/Lincoln_Financial_Field "Lincoln Financial Field") in the [South Philadelphia Sports Complex](/wiki/South_Philadelphia_Sports_Complex "South Philadelphia Sports Complex").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The **Philadelphia Eagles** are a professional [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") team based in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"). The Eagles compete in the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) as a member of the [National Football Conference](/wiki/National_Football_Conference "National Football Conference") (NFC) [East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") division. The team plays its home games at [Lincoln Financial Field](/wiki/Lincoln_Financial_Field "Lincoln Financial Field") in the [South Philadelphia Sports Complex](/wiki/South_Philadelphia_Sports_Complex "South Philadelphia Sports Complex").[[8]](#cite_note-8)
The franchise was established in 1933 as a replacement for the bankrupt [Frankford Yellow Jackets](/wiki/Frankford_Yellow_Jackets "Frankford Yellow Jackets") when a group led by [Bert Bell](/wiki/Bert_Bell "Bert Bell") secured the rights to an NFL franchise in Philadelphia. Since their formation, the Eagles have appeared in the [playoffs](/wiki/NFL_playoffs "NFL playoffs") 32 times, won 17 division titles (including 14 in the NFC East), appeared in four pre-[merger](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_merger "AFL–NFL merger") [NFL Championship Games](/wiki/NFL_Championship_Game "NFL Championship Game"), winning three of them ([1948](/wiki/1948_NFL_Championship_Game "1948 NFL Championship Game"), [1949](/wiki/1949_NFL_Championship_Game "1949 NFL Championship Game"), and [1960](/wiki/1960_NFL_Championship_Game "1960 NFL Championship Game")), and appeared in five [Super Bowls](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), winning Super Bowls [LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII") and [LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX").

The Philadelphia Eagles rank among the best teams in the NFL for attendance and have sold out every home game continuously since the [1999 season](/wiki/1999_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1999 Philadelphia Eagles season").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The Eagles are owned by [Jeffrey Lurie](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lurie "Jeffrey Lurie"), who bought the team in 1994 for $185 million.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11) In December 2024, the Eagles became one of the first teams in the NFL to sell an ownership stake to outside investors. The deal sold 8% of the franchise at a valuation of $8.3 billion.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11)

## History

Main article: [History of the Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/History_of_the_Philadelphia_Eagles "History of the Philadelphia Eagles")

### NFL in Philadelphia (1899–1931)

Main article: [Frankford Yellow Jackets](/wiki/Frankford_Yellow_Jackets "Frankford Yellow Jackets")

The [Frankford Athletic Association](/wiki/Frankford_Yellow_Jackets "Frankford Yellow Jackets") was organized in May 1899 in the parlor of the Suburban Club. The cost of purchasing a share in the association was $10. However, there also were contributing memberships, ranging from $1 to $2.50, made available to the general public. The Association was a community-based non-profit organization of local residents and businesses. In keeping with its charter, which stated that "all profits shall be donated to charity", all of the team's excess income was donated to local charitable institutions. The original Frankford Athletic Association apparently disbanded prior to the 1909 football season. Several of the original players from the 1899 football team kept the team together, and they became known as Loyola Athletic Club. In keeping with Yellow Jackets tradition, they carried the "Frankford" name again in 1912, to become the Frankford Athletic Association.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In the early 1920s, the Frankford Athletic Association's Yellow Jackets gained a reputation as being one of the best independent football teams in the nation. In 1922, Frankford absorbed the Philadelphia City Champion team, the Union Quakers of Philadelphia. That year, Frankford captured the unofficial championship of Philadelphia. During the 1922 and 1923 seasons, the Yellow Jackets compiled a 6–2–1 record against teams from the National Football League. This led to the Association being granted an NFL franchise in 1924, thus becoming the [Frankford Yellow Jackets](/wiki/Frankford_Yellow_Jackets "Frankford Yellow Jackets"). In spite of winning the NFL championship in 1926, midway through the [1931 season](/wiki/1931_NFL_season "1931 NFL season"), the Yellow Jackets went bankrupt and were forced to cease operations.[[13]](#cite_note-Lyons,_2010_pg._81-13)

### Bell and Wray era (1933–1940)

See also: [Bert Bell](/wiki/Bert_Bell "Bert Bell") and [Lud Wray](/wiki/Lud_Wray "Lud Wray")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Grand_Stand_Entrance_of_Shibe_Park.jpg/250px-Grand_Stand_Entrance_of_Shibe_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Stand_Entrance_of_Shibe_Park.jpg)

[Shibe Park](/wiki/Shibe_Park "Shibe Park"), the Eagles' home field, which they shared with baseball's [Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") in 1940 and then from 1942 to 1957

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Aerial_view_of_Municipal_Stadium%2C_Philadelphia%2C_Pa_%2865080%29_%28cropped1%29.jpg/250px-Aerial_view_of_Municipal_Stadium%2C_Philadelphia%2C_Pa_%2865080%29_%28cropped1%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_Municipal_Stadium,_Philadelphia,_Pa_(65080)_(cropped1).jpg)

[Philadelphia Municipal Stadium](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Stadium_(Philadelphia) "John F. Kennedy Stadium (Philadelphia)"), the Eagles' home field from 1936 to 1939 and again in 1941

After more than a year of searching for a suitable replacement for the Yellow Jackets in the lucrative Philadelphia market, the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") granted an expansion franchise to an ownership group headed by [Bert Bell](/wiki/Bert_Bell "Bert Bell") and [Lud Wray](/wiki/Lud_Wray "Lud Wray"), who were also awarded the liquidated assets of the defunct Yellow Jackets organization. The Bell–Wray group had to pay an entry fee of $3,500, or roughly US$67,000 in inflation-adjusted 2022 dollars and assumed a total debt of $11,000 the Yellow Jackets owed to three other NFL franchises.[[14]](#cite_note-Lyons,_2010_pg._82-14) Drawing inspiration from the Blue Eagle logo of the [National Recovery Administration](/wiki/National_Recovery_Administration "National Recovery Administration"), a centerpiece of President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt")'s [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") policies,[[14]](#cite_note-Lyons,_2010_pg._82-14) Bell and Wray named their new franchise the Philadelphia Eagles. While it could seem as if the Yellow Jackets simply rebranded as the Eagles, both the Eagles organization and the NFL officially regard the teams as two separate entities. Not only was there no Philadelphia NFL team for a season and a half, but almost no players from the 1931 Yellow Jackets appeared on the Eagles' first roster.

In the [1933 NFL season](/wiki/1933_NFL_season "1933 NFL season"), the Eagles were one of three teams, along with the Pittsburgh Pirates (now the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers")) and the now-defunct [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds_(NFL) "Cincinnati Reds (NFL)"), to join the NFL as [expansion teams](/wiki/Expansion_team "Expansion team"). Wray became the Eagles' first head coach after being persuaded to assume the position by Bell, his former teammate at [Penn](/wiki/Penn_Quakers_football "Penn Quakers football"). The Eagles originally intended to play their home games at Shibe Park, which was the home of the [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/History_of_the_Philadelphia_Athletics "History of the Philadelphia Athletics") professional baseball in Philadelphia. When negotiations for the use of Shibe Park fell through, however, the Eagles struck a deal with the Athletics' crosstown rival, the [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies"), to begin playing at the [Baker Bowl](/wiki/Baker_Bowl "Baker Bowl").

The Eagles played their first game on October 15, 1933, against the [New York Giants](/wiki/1933_New_York_Giants_season "1933 New York Giants season") at the [Polo Grounds](/wiki/Polo_Grounds "Polo Grounds") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and lost the game 56–0.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The Eagles struggled over the course of their first decade, never winning more than four games in any of their first ten seasons. Their best finish was in [1934](/wiki/1934_NFL_season "1934 NFL season"), the second season for the Eagles, when they tied for third in the East. The Eagles' early rosters largely consisted of former Penn, [Temple](/wiki/Temple_Owls_football "Temple Owls football"), and [Villanova](/wiki/Villanova_Wildcats_football "Villanova Wildcats football") players who played for the Eagles for a few years before going on to other things.

In [1935](/wiki/1935_NFL_season "1935 NFL season"), Bell proposed an annual [college draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft") to equalize talent across the league. The draft was a revolutionary concept in professional sports. Having teams select players in inverse order of their finish in the standings, a practice still followed today, strove to increase fan interest by guaranteeing that even the worst teams would have the opportunity for annual infusions of the best college talent.[[16]](#cite_note-NFL_Chronology_p._351-16) Between [1927](/wiki/1927_NFL_season "1927 NFL season"), when the NFL changed from a sprawling Midwestern-based association to a narrower, major-market league, and 1934, three teams, the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears"), [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants"), and [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers"), won all but one title with the exception of the [Providence Steam Roller](/wiki/Providence_Steam_Roller "Providence Steam Roller"), which won in 1928. By [1936](/wiki/1936_NFL_season "1936 NFL season"), the Eagles suffered significant financial losses and were sold through a public auction. Bert Bell was the only bidder and became the sole owner of the team. Wray refused a reduction in his salary and left the team. Bell assumed the head coaching position and led the team to a record of 1–11, last place in the league.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

From 1936 to 1939, the Eagles played at [Municipal Stadium](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Stadium_(Philadelphia) "John F. Kennedy Stadium (Philadelphia)") in [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia"). In 1940, Bell balked at a 66% rent increase plus 10% of the gate receipts proposed by the City of Philadelphia for the use of Municipal Stadium and signed a lease for Shibe Park, which was renamed [Connie Mack Stadium](/wiki/Connie_Mack_Stadium "Connie Mack Stadium") in 1954. At Connie Mack Stadium, the Eagles were also able to play night games, since lights were installed at the stadium the year before.[[18]](#cite_note-ShibeMove-18) In the 1941 season, the Eagles played their home opener at Municipal Stadium, and then moved to Shibe Park.[[19]](#cite_note-1941Opener-19)[[20]](#cite_note-PHLPIT-20)[[21]](#cite_note-PHLCHI-21) To accommodate football at Shibe Park during the winter, management erected stands in right field, parallel to 20th Street. Some 20 feet high, these east stands included 22 rows of seats. The goalposts stood along the first base line and in left field. The uncovered east stands enlarged the park's capacity to over 39,000, but the Eagles rarely drew more than 25,000 to 30,000.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The Eagles finished the 1937 season 2–8–1 and continued to struggle over the next three seasons.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Thompson, Wolman and the Happy Hundred era (1941–1969)

Further information: [Happy Hundred](/wiki/Happy_Hundred "Happy Hundred")

In December 1940, Bell intervened to stop the sale of [Art Rooney](/wiki/Art_Rooney "Art Rooney")'s [Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") to [Alexis Thompson](/wiki/Alexis_Thompson_(American_football_owner) "Alexis Thompson (American football owner)"),[[24]](#cite_note-Algeo:_16-24) and Rooney then acquired half of Bell's interest in the Eagles.[[25]](#cite_note-25) In a series of events known as the [Pennsylvania Polka](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Polka_(American_football) "Pennsylvania Polka (American football)"),[[24]](#cite_note-Algeo:_16-24) Rooney and Bell exchanged their entire Eagles roster and their territorial rights in Philadelphia to Thompson for his entire Steelers roster and his rights in [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Rooney provided assistance to Bell by rewarding him with a 20% commission on the sale of the Steelers.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Bell became the Steelers' head coach and Rooney became the Steelers' general manager.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

#### Greasy Neale years (1941–1950)

Further information: [1947 NFL Championship Game](/wiki/1947_NFL_Championship_Game "1947 NFL Championship Game"), [1948 NFL Championship Game](/wiki/1948_NFL_Championship_Game "1948 NFL Championship Game"), [1949 NFL Championship Game](/wiki/1949_NFL_Championship_Game "1949 NFL Championship Game"), and [Greasy Neale](/wiki/Greasy_Neale "Greasy Neale")

After assuming ownership, Thompson promptly hired [Greasy Neale](/wiki/Greasy_Neale "Greasy Neale") as the team's head coach. During the first years under Neale, the Eagles' struggles continued, and they finished the [1941 season](/wiki/1941_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1941 Philadelphia Eagles season") with a 2–8–1 record.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In the [1942 season](/wiki/1942_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1942 Philadelphia Eagles season"), there was no improvement as the team finished the season 2–9.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

##### Steagles (1943)

Main article: [Steagles](/wiki/Steagles "Steagles")

Further information: [1943 Philadelphia Eagles season](/wiki/1943_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1943 Philadelphia Eagles season") and [1944 Philadelphia Eagles season](/wiki/1944_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1944 Philadelphia Eagles season")

In [1943](/wiki/1943_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1943 Philadelphia Eagles season"), with player shortages stemming from the U.S. entry engagement in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it became difficult to fill the roster, and the team merged with the Steelers to form the "Phil-Pitt Combine", known as the [Steagles](/wiki/Steagles "Steagles").[[31]](#cite_note-c806-31) Greasy Neale continued to coach the team along with Steelers head coach [Walt Kiesling](/wiki/Walt_Kiesling "Walt Kiesling"). The team finished the 1943 season with a 5–4–1 record, and the merger, which was never intended to be a permanent arrangement, was dissolved at the season's end.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In [1944](/wiki/1944_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1944 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles, led by head coach Greasy Neale and running back [Steve Van Buren](/wiki/Steve_Van_Buren "Steve Van Buren"), had their first winning season in team history.[[33]](#cite_note-33) After two second place finishes in [1945](/wiki/1945_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1945 Philadelphia Eagles season") and [1946](/wiki/1946_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1946 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the team reached the [NFL Championship game](/wiki/1947_NFL_Championship_Game "1947 NFL Championship Game") for the first time in [1947](/wiki/1947_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1947 Philadelphia Eagles season"). Van Buren, [Pete Pihos](/wiki/Pete_Pihos "Pete Pihos"), and [Bosh Pritchard](/wiki/Bosh_Pritchard "Bosh Pritchard") fought valiantly, but the young team lost to the [Chicago Cardinals](/wiki/1947_Chicago_Cardinals_season "1947 Chicago Cardinals season"), 28–21 at [Comiskey Park](/wiki/Comiskey_Park "Comiskey Park") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

##### NFL champions (1948)

Further information: [1948 NFL Championship Game](/wiki/1948_NFL_Championship_Game "1948 NFL Championship Game") and [1948 Philadelphia Eagles season](/wiki/1948_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1948 Philadelphia Eagles season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Van_Buren_1952_Bowman.jpg/250px-Van_Buren_1952_Bowman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Van_Buren_1952_Bowman.jpg)

[Steve Van Buren](/wiki/Steve_Van_Buren "Steve Van Buren"), Eagles halfback from 1944 to 1951, was inducted into the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") in 1965.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/ChuckBednarik1952Bowman.jpg/250px-ChuckBednarik1952Bowman.jpg)](/wiki/File:ChuckBednarik1952Bowman.jpg)

[Chuck Bednarik](/wiki/Chuck_Bednarik "Chuck Bednarik"), Eagles linebacker and center from 1949 to 1962, was inducted into [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") in 1967. His tackle of [Frank Gifford](/wiki/Frank_Gifford "Frank Gifford"), then a running back for the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants"), in November 1960, is widely considered one of the hardest [hits](/wiki/The_Hit_(Chuck_Bednarik) "The Hit (Chuck Bednarik)") and greatest plays in NFL history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/PetePihos1955Bowman.jpg/250px-PetePihos1955Bowman.jpg)](/wiki/File:PetePihos1955Bowman.jpg)

[Pete Pihos](/wiki/Pete_Pihos "Pete Pihos"), Eagles tight end from 1947 to 1955, was inducted into the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") in 1970.

Undeterred, the young team rebounded in [1948](/wiki/1948_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1948 Philadelphia Eagles season") to return to the [NFL Championship game](/wiki/1948_NFL_Championship_Game "1948 NFL Championship Game"). With home-field advantage and a blinding snowstorm on their side, the Eagles won their first NFL Championship against the [Chicago Cardinals](/wiki/1948_Chicago_Cardinals_season "1948 Chicago Cardinals season") by a score of 7–0.[[35]](#cite_note-i638-35) The only score came in the fourth quarter when Steve Van Buren ran for a five-yard touchdown.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Because of the severe weather, few fans witnessed the joyous occasion.

Prior to the start of the [1949 season](/wiki/1949_NFL_season "1949 NFL season"), the Eagles were sold by Thompson to a syndicate of 100 buyers, known as the "[Happy Hundred](/wiki/Happy_Hundred "Happy Hundred")", each of whom paid $3,000 for a share of the team. While the leader of the "Happy Hundred" was noted Philadelphia businessman [James P. Clark](/wiki/James_P._Clark "James P. Clark"), one unsung investor was [Leonard Tose](/wiki/Leonard_Tose "Leonard Tose").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

##### NFL champions (1949)

Further information: [1949 NFL Championship Game](/wiki/1949_NFL_Championship_Game "1949 NFL Championship Game")

In 1949, the Eagles returned to the [NFL Championship game](/wiki/1949_NFL_Championship_Game "1949 NFL Championship Game") for a third consecutive year. The Eagles were favored by a touchdown,[[38]](#cite_note-eshpfv-38)[[39]](#cite_note-rpfuoe-39)[[40]](#cite_note-erbfttd-40) and won 14–0 for their second consecutive title game shutout. Running back [Steve Van Buren](/wiki/Steve_Van_Buren "Steve Van Buren") rushed for 196 yards on 31 carries for the Eagles, and their defense held the Rams to just 21 yards on the ground.[[41]](#cite_note-ertbrir-41) [Chuck Bednarik](/wiki/Chuck_Bednarik "Chuck Bednarik") was selected as the first overall pick in the [1949 NFL draft](/wiki/1949_NFL_draft "1949 NFL draft"). An All-American linebacker from the University of Pennsylvania, Bednarik would go on to become one of the greatest and most beloved players in Eagles history.

In [1950](/wiki/1950_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1950 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles opened the season against the [AAFC](/wiki/All-America_Football_Conference "All-America Football Conference") champion [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns"), who, along with two other AAFC franchises, had just [joined the NFL](/wiki/1950_NFL_season#The_AAFC–NFL_merger "1950 NFL season"). The Eagles were expected to make short work of the Browns, who were widely considered the dominant team in a lesser league. However, the Browns lit up the Eagles' vaunted defense for 487 total yards, including 246 passing yards, in a 35–10 rout. The Eagles never recovered from the loss and finished the 1950 season 6–6.

Following the 1950 season, Greasy Neale retired and was replaced by [Bo McMillin](/wiki/Bo_McMillin "Bo McMillin"). Two games into the [1951 season](/wiki/1951_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1951 Philadelphia Eagles season"), McMillin was forced to retire following a diagnosis of terminal [stomach cancer](/wiki/Stomach_cancer "Stomach cancer"). [Wayne Millner](/wiki/Wayne_Millner "Wayne Millner") finished out the season before being replaced by [Jim Trimble](/wiki/Jim_Trimble "Jim Trimble").

While the remnants of the great 1940s teams managed to stay competitive for the first few years of the decade, and younger players like [Bobby Walston](/wiki/Bobby_Walston "Bobby Walston") and [Sonny Jurgensen](/wiki/Sonny_Jurgensen "Sonny Jurgensen") occasionally provided infusions of talent, the team lacked the total talent necessary for true greatness during most of the 1950s.

After the [1957 season](/wiki/1957_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1957 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles moved from Connie Mack Stadium to [Franklin Field](/wiki/Franklin_Field "Franklin Field") at the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania"). Franklin Field had a vastly expanded seating capacity for the Eagles. While Connie Mack Stadium had a capacity of 39,000, Franklin Field's capacity was 60,000.[[42]](#cite_note-The_Eagles_Encyclopedia-42) In 1969, the grass field at Franklin Field was replaced by [AstroTurf](/wiki/AstroTurf "AstroTurf"), making Franklin Field the first NFL stadium to use artificial turf.

#### Buck Shaw years (1958–1960)

Further information: [1960 NFL Championship Game](/wiki/1960_NFL_Championship_Game "1960 NFL Championship Game") and [Buck Shaw](/wiki/Buck_Shaw "Buck Shaw")

In [1958](/wiki/1958_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1958 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles took several steps to improve, hiring [Buck Shaw](/wiki/Buck_Shaw "Buck Shaw") as head coach and acquiring quarterback [Norm Van Brocklin](/wiki/Norm_Van_Brocklin "Norm Van Brocklin") in a trade with the [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams"). During the [1959 season](/wiki/1959_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1959 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the team showed flashes of talent, and finished in second place in the Eastern Division. Former Eagles owner and co-founder Bert Bell had become [NFL commissioner](/wiki/National_Football_League_Commissioner "National Football League Commissioner"), and attended the Eagles game on October 11 at Franklin Field against the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers"), a team Bell used to own. Bell refused the box seats that the Eagles reserved for him and purchased his own tickets to sit with the fans. During the fourth quarter, while sitting behind the end zone, Bell suffered a heart attack and died later that day.

##### NFL champions (1960)

Further information: [1960 NFL Championship Game](/wiki/1960_NFL_Championship_Game "1960 NFL Championship Game"), [Philadelphia Eagles Santa Claus incident](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles_Santa_Claus_incident "Philadelphia Eagles Santa Claus incident"), and [The Hit (Chuck Bednarik)](/wiki/The_Hit_(Chuck_Bednarik) "The Hit (Chuck Bednarik)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Penn_-_Franklin_Field_-_1922.jpg/250px-Penn_-_Franklin_Field_-_1922.jpg)](/wiki/File:Penn_-_Franklin_Field_-_1922.jpg)

[Franklin Field](/wiki/Franklin_Field "Franklin Field"), the Eagles' home field from 1958 to 1970

The Eagles' [1960 season](/wiki/1960_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1960 Philadelphia Eagles season") remains one of the most celebrated years in team history. Shaw, Van Brocklin, and [Bednarik](/wiki/Chuck_Bednarik "Chuck Bednarik"), each in their last season before retirement, led an Eagles team more notable for its grit than its talent. One observer later quipped that the team had "nothing but a championship" to its first division title since 1949. The team was aided by their two Pro Bowl receivers, wide receiver [Tommy McDonald](/wiki/Tommy_McDonald_(American_football) "Tommy McDonald (American football)"), who later wrote an autobiography titled *They Pay Me to Catch Footballs*, and tight end [Pete Retzlaff](/wiki/Pete_Retzlaff "Pete Retzlaff").

On November 20, 1960, at [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium_(1923) "Yankee Stadium (1923)") in [The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), Bednarik launched a tackle against [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants")' running back [Frank Gifford](/wiki/Frank_Gifford "Frank Gifford"), which has come to be known as [The Hit](/wiki/The_Hit_(Chuck_Bednarik) "The Hit (Chuck Bednarik)"), a tackle that is routinely ranked as one of the hardest and most vicious hits in [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") history. With the game tied 10 to 10 in the fourth quarter, Gifford caught a short pass over the middle and was immediately hit by Bednarik with a clothesline tackle so hard that it dropped Gifford to the ground unconscious. Gifford was removed from the field on a stretcher and transported to a local hospital by ambulance, where he remained for ten days. Gifford was diagnosed with a deep [concussion](/wiki/Concussion "Concussion") that resulted in his retirement from the game for 18 months.[[43]](#cite_note-43) On the 100th anniversary of the NFL's founding, the NFL ranked Bednarik's tackle the 44th greatest play in league history.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

On December 26, 1960, one of the coldest days in recorded Philadelphia history, the Eagles faced [Vince Lombardi](/wiki/Vince_Lombardi "Vince Lombardi")'s [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") in the [1960 NFL Championship Game](/wiki/1960_NFL_Championship_Game "1960 NFL Championship Game") and dealt the formidable Lombardi the sole championship game loss of his storied career. Bednarik lined up at center on offense and at linebacker on defense. Fittingly, the game ended as Bednarik tackled a struggling [Jim Taylor](/wiki/Jim_Taylor_(fullback) "Jim Taylor (fullback)") and refused to allow him to stand until the last seconds had ticked away.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Van Brocklin came to Philadelphia and agreed to play through 1960 with an understanding that, upon his retirement as a player, he would succeed Shaw as head coach. Ownership, however, opted to promote assistant coach [Nick Skorich](/wiki/Nick_Skorich "Nick Skorich") instead, and Van Brocklin quit the organization in a fit of pique, instead becoming head coach of the [expansion](/wiki/1961_NFL_expansion_draft "1961 NFL expansion draft") [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/1961_Minnesota_Vikings_season "1961 Minnesota Vikings season"). Backup quarterback [Sonny Jurgensen](/wiki/Sonny_Jurgensen "Sonny Jurgensen") became the Eagles' starter for the [1961 season](/wiki/1961_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1961 Philadelphia Eagles season"); they finished a half-game behind the [New York Giants](/wiki/1961_New_York_Giants_season "1961 New York Giants season") for first place in the Eastern Conference standings with a 10–4 record. Despite the success, however, the franchise was in turmoil.

The [1962 team](/wiki/1962_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1962 Philadelphia Eagles season"), decimated by injury, managed only three wins and were embarrassed at home in a 49–0 loss to the [Packers](/wiki/1962_Green_Bay_Packers_season "1962 Green Bay Packers season"). The off-field chaos continued through [1963](/wiki/1963_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1963 Philadelphia Eagles season"), as the 65 shareholders remaining from the original Happy Hundred sold the team to [Jerry Wolman](/wiki/Jerry_Wolman "Jerry Wolman"), a 36-year-old millionaire Washington developer who outbid local bidders for the team, paying an unprecedented $5.505 million for control of the club.[[46]](#cite_note-ppegpff-46)[[47]](#cite_note-tbapegbt-47)

In [1964](/wiki/1964_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1964 Philadelphia Eagles season"), Wolman hired former Cardinals and [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins") coach [Joe Kuharich](/wiki/Joe_Kuharich "Joe Kuharich") to a 15-year contract. Over the next five seasons, the team failed to make the playoffs and the failures of the team over this period were highlighted by the [Santa Claus incident](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles_Santa_Claus_incident "Philadelphia Eagles Santa Claus incident"), when fans pelted a Santa Claus with snowballs during a halftime Christmas pageant. The team had only one winning season, in [1966](/wiki/1966_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1966 Philadelphia Eagles season"), finishing second in the [NFL Eastern Conference](/wiki/Eastern_and_Western_Conferences_(NFL)_1933%E2%80%931969 "Eastern and Western Conferences (NFL) 1933–1969"). The Eagles lost to the [Baltimore Colts](/wiki/Baltimore_Colts "Baltimore Colts") 20–14, in the post-season's third place consolation game, the [Playoff Bowl](/wiki/Playoff_Bowl "Playoff Bowl"), which was held at the [Orange Bowl](/wiki/Miami_Orange_Bowl "Miami Orange Bowl") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"). The Eagles finished 6–7–1 in [1967](/wiki/1967_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1967 Philadelphia Eagles season"). In the following season, the Eagles fell even further in the standings, recording a 2–12 record in [1968](/wiki/1968_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1968 Philadelphia Eagles season").

### Leonard Tose era (1969–1984)

See also: [Leonard Tose](/wiki/Leonard_Tose "Leonard Tose")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/b5/VeteransStadium1986-2a.jpg/250px-VeteransStadium1986-2a.jpg)](/wiki/File:VeteransStadium1986-2a.jpg)

[Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium") in [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia"), the Eagles' home field from [1971](/wiki/1971_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1971 Philadelphia Eagles season") to [2002](/wiki/2002_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2002 Philadelphia Eagles season"), which they shared with baseball's [Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies")

In [1969](/wiki/1969_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1969 Philadelphia Eagles season"), [Leonard Tose](/wiki/Leonard_Tose "Leonard Tose") bought the team from Wolman for $16.155 million,[[48]](#cite_note-48) the equivalent of $142 million today, representing a record then for the highest amount ever paid for a professional sports franchise. Tose's first official act was to fire Coach [Joe Kuharich](/wiki/Joe_Kuharich "Joe Kuharich") after a disappointing 24–41–1 record during his five-year reign. Tose then named former Eagles wide receiver [Pete Retzlaff](/wiki/Pete_Retzlaff "Pete Retzlaff") as the team's general manager and [Jerry Williams](/wiki/Jerry_Williams_(American_football) "Jerry Williams (American football)") as its new head coach.

With the [merger](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_merger "AFL–NFL merger") of the NFL and [AFL](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League") in [1970](/wiki/1970_NFL_season "1970 NFL season"), the Eagles were placed in the [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") Division with the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants"), [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins"), and [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys"). The Eagles' heated [rivalry](/wiki/Eagles%E2%80%93Giants_rivalry "Eagles–Giants rivalry") with the Giants is the oldest of the NFC East rivalries, dating back to 1933, and is often cited as one of the best rivalries in the NFL.[[49]](#cite_note-Giants-Eagles_rivalry-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) [1970](/wiki/1970_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1970 Philadelphia Eagles season") was also the last season for the Eagles at Franklin Field; the team finished the first post-merger season in last place in their division at 3–10–1.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In [1971](/wiki/1971_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1971 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles moved to [Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium"), which had just been constructed and was initially acclaimed as a triumph of ultra-modern sports engineering.[[53]](#cite_note-w709-53) After a 3–10–1 record in 1970 and three consecutive blowout losses to [Cincinnati](/wiki/1971_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "1971 Cincinnati Bengals season"), [Dallas](/wiki/1971_Dallas_Cowboys_season "1971 Dallas Cowboys season"), and [San Francisco](/wiki/1971_San_Francisco_49ers_season "1971 San Francisco 49ers season") to open the [1971](/wiki/1971_NFL_season "1971 NFL season") season, Williams was fired and replaced by assistant coach Ed Khayat, a defensive lineman on the Eagles' 1960 NFL championship team. Williams and Khayat were hampered by Retzlaff's decision to trade longtime starting quarterback [Norm Snead](/wiki/Norm_Snead "Norm Snead") to the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/1971_Minnesota_Vikings_season "1971 Minnesota Vikings season") in early 1971, leaving the Eagles a choice between journeyman [Pete Liske](/wiki/Pete_Liske "Pete Liske") and raw [Rick Arrington](/wiki/Rick_Arrington "Rick Arrington"). Khayat lost his first two games but won six of the season's last nine, thanks largely to the efforts of the Eagles' defense, led by all-pro safety [Bill Bradley](/wiki/Bill_Bradley_(American_football) "Bill Bradley (American football)"), who led the NFL in interceptions (11) and interception return yardage (248).

The team regressed in [1972](/wiki/1972_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1972 Philadelphia Eagles season") to finish 2–11–1, and Khayat was released. The two wins (both on the road) proved to be surprises, however. Philadelphia beat the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/1972_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "1972 Kansas City Chiefs season") (which had had the best record in the AFC a year before) 21–20 and the [Houston Oilers](/wiki/1972_Houston_Oilers_season "1972 Houston Oilers season") 18–17 on six field goals by kicker [Tom Dempsey](/wiki/Tom_Dempsey "Tom Dempsey"). The latter game had been called the "Johnny Rodgers Bowl", because the loser, in finishing last in the league, would gain the first overall pick in the [1973 NFL draft](/wiki/1973_NFL_draft "1973 NFL draft"), which was then presumed to be [Nebraska](/wiki/1972_Nebraska_Cornhuskers_football_team "1972 Nebraska Cornhuskers football team") wingback [Johnny Rodgers](/wiki/Johnny_Rodgers "Johnny Rodgers"), the [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy") [winner](/wiki/1972_NCAA_University_Division_football_season#Heisman_Trophy "1972 NCAA University Division football season"). With their loss, the Oilers got first pick and took [University of Tampa](/wiki/1972_Tampa_Spartans_football_team "1972 Tampa Spartans football team") defensive end [John Matuszak](/wiki/John_Matuszak "John Matuszak"), who later faced Philadelphia in [Super Bowl XV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XV "Super Bowl XV"). With the second overall pick, the Eagles selected [USC](/wiki/1972_USC_Trojans_football_team "1972 USC Trojans football team") tight end [Charle Young](/wiki/Charle_Young "Charle Young").

Khayat was replaced by offensive guru [Mike McCormack](/wiki/Mike_McCormack_(American_football) "Mike McCormack (American football)") for the [1973 season](/wiki/1973_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1973 Philadelphia Eagles season"). Aided by the skills of quarterback [Roman Gabriel](/wiki/Roman_Gabriel "Roman Gabriel") and towering young wide receiver [Harold Carmichael](/wiki/Harold_Carmichael "Harold Carmichael"), they managed to infuse a bit of vitality into a previously moribund offense.

New general manager [Jim Murray](/wiki/Jim_Murray_(American_football) "Jim Murray (American football)") also began to add talent on the defensive side of the line, most notably through the addition of future Pro Bowl linebacker [Bill Bergey](/wiki/Bill_Bergey "Bill Bergey") in [1974](/wiki/1974_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1974 Philadelphia Eagles season"). Overall, however, the team was still mired in mediocrity. McCormack was fired after a 4–10 [1975 season](/wiki/1975_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1975 Philadelphia Eagles season").

#### Dick Vermeil years (1976–1982)

See also: [Miracle at the Meadowlands](/wiki/Miracle_at_the_Meadowlands "Miracle at the Meadowlands") and [Super Bowl XV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XV "Super Bowl XV")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Dickvermeil.jpg/250px-Dickvermeil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dickvermeil.jpg)

[Dick Vermeil](/wiki/Dick_Vermeil "Dick Vermeil"), Eagles head coach from 1976 to 1982, who led the Eagles to their first [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") appearance in [Super Bowl XV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XV "Super Bowl XV") against the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") in 1981

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/1986_Jeno%27s_Pizza_-_05_-_Wilbert_Montgomery.jpg/250px-1986_Jeno%27s_Pizza_-_05_-_Wilbert_Montgomery.jpg)](/wiki/File:1986_Jeno%27s_Pizza_-_05_-_Wilbert_Montgomery.jpg)

[Wilbert Montgomery](/wiki/Wilbert_Montgomery "Wilbert Montgomery"), Eagles running back from 1977 to 1984, in the [1980 NFC Championship Game](/wiki/1980_NFC_Championship_Game "1980 NFC Championship Game") against the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") at [Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium")

In [1976](/wiki/1976_NFL_season "1976 NFL season"), [Dick Vermeil](/wiki/Dick_Vermeil "Dick Vermeil") was hired from [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA_Bruins_football "UCLA Bruins football") to coach the struggling Eagles, who had managed only one winning season from 1962 to 1975.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Vermeil faced numerous obstacles as he attempted to rejuvenate a franchise that had not seriously contended in well over a decade. Despite the team's young talent and Gabriel's occasional flashes of brilliance, the Eagles finished [1976](/wiki/1976_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1976 Philadelphia Eagles season") with the same 4–10 record as in 1975. In [1977](/wiki/1977_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1977 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the first seeds of hope began to emerge. The team obtained hard-throwing quarterback [Ron Jaworski](/wiki/Ron_Jaworski "Ron Jaworski") in a trade from the [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams") in exchange for popular tight end [Charle Young](/wiki/Charle_Young "Charle Young"). The defense, led by Bergey and defensive coordinator [Marion Campbell](/wiki/Marion_Campbell "Marion Campbell"), began earning a reputation as one of the hardest-hitting in the league.

[1978](/wiki/1978_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1978 Philadelphia Eagles season") saw one of the great moments in Eagles history, [The Miracle at the Meadowlands](/wiki/The_Miracle_at_the_Meadowlands "The Miracle at the Meadowlands"), when [Herman Edwards](/wiki/Herman_Edwards "Herman Edwards") returned a fumble by [Giants](/wiki/1978_New_York_Giants_season "1978 New York Giants season")' quarterback [Joe Pisarcik](/wiki/Joe_Pisarcik "Joe Pisarcik") for a touchdown with 20 seconds left in the game, resulting in a 19–17 Eagles victory.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The Eagles would edge into the playoffs with a 9–7 season. Young running back [Wilbert Montgomery](/wiki/Wilbert_Montgomery "Wilbert Montgomery") became the first Eagle since Steve Van Buren to exceed 1,000 yards in a season.[[56]](#cite_note-56) In [1979](/wiki/1979_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1979 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles tied for first place with an 11–5 record, as Montgomery shattered team rushing records with a total of 1,512 yards.

In [1980](/wiki/1980_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1980 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the team dominated the NFC, facing its chief nemesis, the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/1980_Dallas_Cowboys_season "1980 Dallas Cowboys season"), in the [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Philadelphia_Eagles_20,_Dallas_Cowboys_7 "1980–81 NFL playoffs"). The game was played in cold conditions before faithful fans at [Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium"). Led by an outstanding rushing performance by Montgomery, whose long cutback TD run in the first half is one of the most memorable in Eagles history, and a gutsy game from fullback [Leroy Harris](/wiki/Leroy_Harris_(running_back) "Leroy Harris (running back)"), who scored the Eagles' only other TD that day, the Birds earned a berth in [Super Bowl XV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XV "Super Bowl XV") with a 20–7 victory.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

The Eagles traveled to [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") for Super Bowl XV, where they were heavily favored over the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/1980_Oakland_Raiders_season "1980 Oakland Raiders season"), who had squeaked into the playoffs as a wild-card team. Things did not go the Eagles' way, beginning with Tose's imprudent decision to bring comedian [Don Rickles](/wiki/Don_Rickles "Don Rickles") into the pregame locker room to lighten the mood. Jaworski's first pass was intercepted by Rod Martin, setting up an Oakland touchdown. Later in the first quarter, a potential game-tying 40-yard touchdown pass to Rodney Parker was nullified by an illegal-motion penalty. The final score was 27–10. Journeyman quarterback [Jim Plunkett](/wiki/Jim_Plunkett "Jim Plunkett") was named the game's MVP.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

The team got off to a promising start in the [1981 season](/wiki/1981_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1981 Philadelphia Eagles season") by winning their first six games. They ended up 10–6 to earn a wild-card berth. However, their hopes to repeat as NFC champs were dashed in the wild-card round by the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants"), who won 27–21.

After the Eagles finished 3–6 in the strike-shortened [1982 season](/wiki/1982_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1982 Philadelphia Eagles season"), Vermeil quit the team, citing "burnout".

Defensive coordinator Marion Campbell replaced Vermeil as head coach. Campbell had helped to popularize the "bend-don't-break" defensive strategy in the 1970s. Philadelphia struggled through the mid-1980s, marked by flagging fan participation. The team failed to make the playoffs in [1983](/wiki/1983_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1983 Philadelphia Eagles season") and [1984](/wiki/1984_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1984 Philadelphia Eagles season"). The team nearly moved to [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") at the end of the 1984 season.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Norman Braman era (1985–1993)

See also: [Norman Braman](/wiki/Norman_Braman "Norman Braman")

In [1985](/wiki/1985_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1985 Philadelphia Eagles season"), Tose was forced to sell the Eagles to [Norman Braman](/wiki/Norman_Braman "Norman Braman") and Ed Leibowitz, highly successful automobile dealers from Florida, for a reported $65 million (equal to $195 million today) to pay off his more than $25 million ($75 million today) in gambling debts at [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey") casinos.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The team again struggled during the 1985 season, and Campbell was fired after week 16, to be replaced by assistant head coach/defensive backs coach [Fred Bruney](/wiki/Fred_Bruney "Fred Bruney") for the season's last game.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

In the 1985 Supplemental draft, the Eagles acquired the rights to the [Memphis Showboats](/wiki/Memphis_Showboats "Memphis Showboats")' elite pass rusher [Reggie White](/wiki/Reggie_White "Reggie White").[[62]](#cite_note-p486-62)

#### Buddy Ryan years (1986–1990)

See also: [Body Bag Game](/wiki/Body_Bag_Game "Body Bag Game"), [Bounty Bowl](/wiki/Bounty_Bowl "Bounty Bowl"), and [Buddy Ryan](/wiki/Buddy_Ryan "Buddy Ryan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/7/71/Jerome_Brown_of_the_Philadelphia_Eagles_%28ca._1991%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jerome_Brown_of_the_Philadelphia_Eagles_(ca._1991).jpg)

[Jerome Brown](/wiki/Jerome_Brown "Jerome Brown"), Eagles defensive end from 1987 to 1991, was named to the 1990 and 1991 [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") teams before dying in a tragic car accident in June 1992 at age 27.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Reggie_White_HOF_jersey.jpg/250px-Reggie_White_HOF_jersey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reggie_White_HOF_jersey.jpg)

[Reggie White](/wiki/Reggie_White "Reggie White")'s Eagles jersey on display at the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") in [Canton, Ohio](/wiki/Canton,_Ohio "Canton, Ohio")

In [1986](/wiki/1986_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1986 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the arrival of head coach [Buddy Ryan](/wiki/Buddy_Ryan "Buddy Ryan") and his fiery attitude sparked team performance and ignited the fan base. Immediately infusing the team with his hard-as-nails attitude, the Eagles quickly became known for their tough defense and tougher attitudes.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Ryan began rejuvenating the team by releasing several aging players, including Ron Jaworski. [Randall Cunningham](/wiki/Randall_Cunningham "Randall Cunningham") took his place and, despite a 5–10–1 season, began showing considerable promise. [1987](/wiki/1987_NFL_season "1987 NFL season") saw another [strike](/wiki/1987_NFL_strike "1987 NFL strike"), which shortened the season by one game.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The substitutes who had filled in for the strikers performed poorly and were crushed 41–22 by the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys"). After the strike, the regular Eagles' [1987](/wiki/1987_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1987 Philadelphia Eagles season") team won a 37–20 revenge game against Dallas. The season record was 7–8, of which three games had been played by substitutes.

The Eagles reached the [playoffs](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_NFL_playoffs "1988–89 NFL playoffs") in [1988](/wiki/1988_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1988 Philadelphia Eagles season"), but lost to the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears"), the team that Ryan had helped lead to a [Super Bowl XX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XX "Super Bowl XX") victory as defensive coordinator. The game became known as the "[Fog Bowl](/wiki/Fog_Bowl_(American_football) "Fog Bowl (American football)")", due to the inclement weather during the game. The Eagles lost, 20–12.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

The following two years would see playoff appearances as well, but the team never made it past the first round. This failure was very frustrating to Eagles fans, as the team was widely regarded as among the most talented in the NFL. On offense, the Eagles were led by quarterback Cunningham, one of the most exciting players of his generation; tight end [Keith Jackson](/wiki/Keith_Jackson_(tight_end) "Keith Jackson (tight end)"); and fullback [Keith Byars](/wiki/Keith_Byars "Keith Byars"). The defense is commonly acknowledged as among the greatest in league history, and as the best to never win a championship.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The two [1989](/wiki/1989_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1989 Philadelphia Eagles season") matches with Dallas were known as the Bounty Bowls. Both were won easily by the Eagles (the Cowboys finished 1–15 that year), and were marked by Ryan insulting new Cowboys coach [Jimmy Johnson](/wiki/Jimmy_Johnson_(American_football_coach) "Jimmy Johnson (American football coach)") by placing a bounty on their kicker, and by Eagles fans throwing snowballs at him at Veterans Stadium.[[67]](#cite_note-67) On November 12, 1990, during a *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* game at the Vet, the [Eagles](/wiki/1990_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1990 Philadelphia Eagles season") defeated the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins") by a score of 28–14, as the defense scored three of the team's four touchdowns. This game, more lopsided than its score would indicate, was subsequently labeled [the Body Bag Game](/wiki/The_Body_Bag_Game "The Body Bag Game") in reference to the number of injuries Eagles players inflicted on the Redskins and the physically tough play of the Eagles in the game. In the game, the Eagles knocked out the starting Washington quarterback, and then seriously injured his replacement. Running back [Brian Mitchell](/wiki/Brian_Mitchell_(running_back) "Brian Mitchell (running back)"), who would later be signed by the Eagles, was called upon to finish the game as the Redskins' quarterback.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Washington returned to Philadelphia in the first round of the [playoffs](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_NFL_playoffs "1990–91 NFL playoffs") to defeat the Eagles 20–6, ending their season.

Ryan was fired on January 7, 1991, and was replaced by offensive coordinator [Rich Kotite](/wiki/Rich_Kotite "Rich Kotite").[[69]](#cite_note-69) The team started the [1991 season](/wiki/1991_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1991 Philadelphia Eagles season") with the loss of starting quarterback Randall Cunningham due to a knee injury. Backup quarterback [Jim McMahon](/wiki/Jim_McMahon "Jim McMahon") assumed the starting role for the rest of the season. Despite having the top-rated defense in the league, the team failed to make the playoffs by finishing third in the NFC East with a record of 10–6.

On June 25, 1992, All Pro defensive tackle [Jerome Brown](/wiki/Jerome_Brown "Jerome Brown") was killed in an automobile accident. The team and fan base dedicated the [1992 season](/wiki/1992_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1992 Philadelphia Eagles season") to "bring it home for Jerome".[[70]](#cite_note-70) The team finished second in the NFC East with an 11–5 record to earn a wild card playoff spot. Kotite led the Eagles to a victory over the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/1992_New_Orleans_Saints_season "1992 New Orleans Saints season") in the wild card game but then fell to the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/1992_Dallas_Cowboys_season "1992 Dallas Cowboys season") in the Divisional round. Another blow to the team was the loss of all-time sacks leader Reggie White to free agency in the off-season.

Among the team's offensive stars during that period were quarterback Randall Cunningham, tight end Keith Jackson, and running back [Herschel Walker](/wiki/Herschel_Walker "Herschel Walker"). But what was known as the Gang Green defense, led by [Reggie White](/wiki/Reggie_White "Reggie White"), Jerome Brown, [Clyde Simmons](/wiki/Clyde_Simmons "Clyde Simmons"), [Seth Joyner](/wiki/Seth_Joyner "Seth Joyner"), [Wes Hopkins](/wiki/Wes_Hopkins "Wes Hopkins"), [Mike Golic](/wiki/Mike_Golic "Mike Golic"), [Byron Evans](/wiki/Byron_Evans "Byron Evans"), [Eric Allen](/wiki/Eric_Allen "Eric Allen"), [Andre Waters](/wiki/Andre_Waters "Andre Waters"), and [Mark McMillian](/wiki/Mark_McMillian "Mark McMillian"), defined the team. In [1993](/wiki/1993_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1993 Philadelphia Eagles season"), Kotite's Eagles fell apart after a promising start and miss the playoffs, ending the regular season 8–8.

### Jeffrey Lurie era (1994–present)

See also: [Jeffrey Lurie](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lurie "Jeffrey Lurie")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Jeffrey-lurie-philadelphia-eagles-by-bill-cramer.jpg/250px-Jeffrey-lurie-philadelphia-eagles-by-bill-cramer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeffrey-lurie-philadelphia-eagles-by-bill-cramer.jpg)

Jeffrey Lurie in 2010

By 1994, team owner [Norman Braman](/wiki/Norman_Braman "Norman Braman") had become largely unpopular among local fans and a polarizing presence in the front office. [Jeffrey Lurie](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lurie "Jeffrey Lurie") bought the Eagles on May 6, 1994, for an estimated $185 million.[[71]](#cite_note-k574-71)

In Lurie's first season as owner in [1994](/wiki/1994_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1994 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the team went 7–9 and again missed the playoffs.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Rich Kotite was fired and replaced by [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") defensive coordinator [Ray Rhodes](/wiki/Ray_Rhodes "Ray Rhodes"), who successfully lured 49ers star [Ricky Watters](/wiki/Ricky_Watters "Ricky Watters") to join the team as a free agent.[[73]](#cite_note-m077-73)[[74]](#cite_note-c582-74)

In [1995](/wiki/1995_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1995 Philadelphia Eagles season"), Rhodes's first season, the Eagles got off to a slow start by losing three of their first four games but subsequently rebounded to finish with a 10–6 record and a playoff spot.[[75]](#cite_note-75) In the Wild Card Round, the Eagles, playing at home, overwhelmed the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions") 58–37, with 31 of Philadelphia's points coming in the second quarter.[[76]](#cite_note-76) However, the Eagles were again eliminated in the next round by the Cowboys by a score of 30–11.[[77]](#cite_note-77) This would be [Randall Cunningham](/wiki/Randall_Cunningham "Randall Cunningham")'s last game as an Eagle. Cunningham scored the only touchdown of the game and the last Eagles postseason touchdown for six years.

1995 marked the end of Cunningham's tenure as starting quarterback. Rhodes benched Cunningham in favor of [Rodney Peete](/wiki/Rodney_Peete "Rodney Peete"), leading to friction between the two. Earlier, rumors had circulated that Lurie and Rhodes had tried to trade Cunningham to the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals"). However, no such trade materialized and Cunningham retired shortly after the season.

In [1996](/wiki/1996_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1996 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles' uniform colors were changed from the classic [kelly green](/wiki/Kelly_green "Kelly green") to a darker [midnight green](/wiki/Midnight_green "Midnight green").[[78]](#cite_note-p747-78) The team got off to a good start, winning three of their first four games. However, a week 5 Monday night game at [Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium") against the rival Cowboys witnessed a season ending knee injury to Peete, loss of the team's momentum, and the transition to an offense led by [Ty Detmer](/wiki/Ty_Detmer "Ty Detmer") and Watters. While Detmer played well and Watters rushed for 1,411 yards, the season conformed to what had become a familiar pattern: 10–6 record and early elimination (a 14–0 shutout by the [49ers](/wiki/1996_San_Francisco_49ers_season "1996 San Francisco 49ers season")) in the [playoffs](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_NFL_playoffs "1996–97 NFL playoffs"). In the [1996 NFL draft](/wiki/1996_NFL_draft "1996 NFL draft"), [Brian Dawkins](/wiki/Brian_Dawkins "Brian Dawkins") was chosen in the second round. The continued early playoff exits led fans and local media to cast blame on high-priced free agents ([Irving Fryar](/wiki/Irving_Fryar "Irving Fryar"), Watters, [Troy Vincent](/wiki/Troy_Vincent "Troy Vincent"), and [Guy McIntyre](/wiki/Guy_McIntyre "Guy McIntyre")) for not stepping up in big games, especially in the postseason. Rhodes gradually deteriorated under the stress of the job, and players were beginning to grow tired of his brash demeanor and often autocratic coaching style.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

After a 6–9–1 campaign in [1997](/wiki/1997_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1997 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the bottom fell out in [1998](/wiki/1998_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1998 Philadelphia Eagles season").[[80]](#cite_note-80) The Eagles suffered a 3–13 record, their worst since 1972, and were ranked dead last in numerous offensive statistics.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) Home attendance was declining, a quarterback controversy was deteriorating an already rudderless locker room, and the players had all but tuned out the embattled coaching staff. Left with little choice after a disastrous season, fan revolt, and sagging team morale, Lurie fired Rhodes and hired Green Bay Packers quarterback coach [Andy Reid](/wiki/Andy_Reid "Andy Reid") as head coach.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

#### Andy Reid years (1999–2012)

See also: [Andy Reid](/wiki/Andy_Reid "Andy Reid"), [Super Bowl XXXIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIX "Super Bowl XXXIX"), and [Miracle at the New Meadowlands](/wiki/Miracle_at_the_New_Meadowlands "Miracle at the New Meadowlands")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Andy_Reid_080805-F-9429S-131_crop.jpg/250px-Andy_Reid_080805-F-9429S-131_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andy_Reid_080805-F-9429S-131_crop.jpg)

[Andy Reid](/wiki/Andy_Reid "Andy Reid"), Eagles head coach from 1999 to 2012, led the Eagles to [Super Bowl XXXIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIX "Super Bowl XXXIX") against the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") in 2004.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Bdawk.jpg/250px-Bdawk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bdawk.jpg)

[Brian Dawkins](/wiki/Brian_Dawkins "Brian Dawkins"), Eagles safety from 1996 to 2008, was inducted into the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") in 2018

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Donovan_McNabb.jpg/250px-Donovan_McNabb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donovan_McNabb.jpg)

[Donovan McNabb](/wiki/Donovan_McNabb "Donovan McNabb"), Eagles quarterback from 1999 to 2009 and [Andy Reid](/wiki/Andy_Reid "Andy Reid")'s first draft selection as Eagles head coach in the [1999 NFL draft](/wiki/1999_NFL_draft "1999 NFL draft")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Brian_Westbrook.jpg/250px-Brian_Westbrook.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brian_Westbrook.jpg)

[Brian Westbrook](/wiki/Brian_Westbrook "Brian Westbrook"), Eagles Running Back from 2002 to 2009, and inducted into the Eagles Hall of Fame in 2015.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Desean_Jackson_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Desean_Jackson_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Desean_Jackson_(cropped).jpg)

[DeSean Jackson](/wiki/DeSean_Jackson "DeSean Jackson") played for the Eagles from 2008 to 2013 and then again from 2019 to 2020; his December 19, 2010, punt return against the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants"), known as the [Miracle at the New Meadowlands](/wiki/Miracle_at_the_New_Meadowlands "Miracle at the New Meadowlands"), is considered one of the greatest plays in [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") history.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/LeSean_McCoy_2.jpg/250px-LeSean_McCoy_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:LeSean_McCoy_2.jpg)

[LeSean McCoy](/wiki/LeSean_McCoy "LeSean McCoy"), Eagles running back from 2009 to 2014

The Eagles reemerged as a predominant NFL team under the leadership of new head coach [Andy Reid](/wiki/Andy_Reid "Andy Reid"), who was hired away from the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers"), where he was the Packers' quarterback coach. Reid's first notable step was drafting [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse_University "Syracuse University") quarterback [Donovan McNabb](/wiki/Donovan_McNabb "Donovan McNabb") with the second overall pick in the [1999 NFL draft](/wiki/1999_NFL_draft "1999 NFL draft").[[86]](#cite_note-r345-86) The Eagles' awful [1998 season](/wiki/1998_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1998 Philadelphia Eagles season") would have entitled the Eagles to first pick but it was awarded to the rebooted [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns"). Despite clearing the roster for new talent by releasing unpopular and aging veterans such as [Ricky Watters](/wiki/Ricky_Watters "Ricky Watters") and [Irving Fryar](/wiki/Irving_Fryar "Irving Fryar"), Reid was still a virtual unknown before his arrival as head coach, and his appointment was met initially with skepticism among Philadelphia fans. The drafting of McNabb was unpopular with many Eagles fans, many of whom traveled to [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") for the 1999 Draft and booed the selection, believing the Eagles instead should have selected [Ricky Williams](/wiki/Ricky_Williams "Ricky Williams"), a [University of Texas](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns_football "Texas Longhorns football") [running back](/wiki/Running_back "Running back") who had been named the 1998 [AP College Player of the Year](/wiki/Associated_Press_College_Football_Player_of_the_Year "Associated Press College Football Player of the Year").

The [1999 season](/wiki/1999_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1999 Philadelphia Eagles season") was largely a rebuilding year for the Eagles. The team, which routinely sells out its home games in Philadelphia, had two 1999 home games that failed to sell out, resulting in local [TV blackouts](/wiki/Blackout_(broadcasting) "Blackout (broadcasting)"). Another six home games in the 1999 season sold out only because several local small business owners bought the remaining unsold tickets to spare Philadelphia-area viewers a television blackout of the game.

The Week 5 home game at Veterans Stadium on October 10, 1999, against the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") represented the last play of Cowboys wide receiver [Michael Irvin](/wiki/Michael_Irvin "Michael Irvin")'s career. In the game, Irvin was driven into the turf by Eagles safety [Tim Hauck](/wiki/Tim_Hauck "Tim Hauck"), which left Irvin lying motionless. Some Eagles fans responded with applause, giving further rise to Philadelphia's reputation as unduly aggressive and hostile fans. Irvin was transported from the field by stretcher and then by ambulance to [Thomas Jefferson University Hospital](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_University_Hospital "Thomas Jefferson University Hospital"). Irvin, who was diagnosed with a potentially threatening [cervical spine](/wiki/Cervical_vertebrae "Cervical vertebrae") injury and forced to announce his NFL retirement after the injury, later said the Philadelphia fans were applauding his departure rather than his serious injury.[[87]](#cite_note-87) The team finished the season with a record of 5–11.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

The [2000](/wiki/2000_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2000 Philadelphia Eagles season") regular-season opener in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") on September 3 became known in NFL lore as the "[Pickle Juice Game](/wiki/Pickle_Juice_Game "Pickle Juice Game")". Kickoff temperature in [Texas Stadium](/wiki/Texas_Stadium "Texas Stadium") was 109 degrees Fahrenheit and soared to nearly 120, making it the hottest game in league history, beating a previous record set in a 1997 Cowboys–Cardinals match in Arizona. The nickname came about because an Eagles trainer had prepared for the predicted heat by having the players drink the juice from jars of dill pickles in order to retain body moisture and stave off cramps and heat exhaustion. The experiment proved successful as the Eagles won 41–14 while multiple Cowboys players had been consigned to the bench, unable to handle the torrid climate while the Eagles had no players benched. The game was also significant as marking the beginning of Philadelphia's domination of the NFC East. The team finished the season at 11–5, reaching the [playoffs](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_NFL_playoffs "2000–01 NFL playoffs") as a wild card, which restored fan optimism. After brushing aside the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2000_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2000 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") 21–3, the [Eagles](/wiki/2000_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2000 Philadelphia Eagles season") moved to the second round of the playoffs, only to lose 20–10 to the [New York Giants](/wiki/2000_New_York_Giants_season "2000 New York Giants season"). After compiling an 11–5 record in [2001](/wiki/2001_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2001 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles reached the [playoffs](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_NFL_playoffs "2001–02 NFL playoffs") again, this time at the top of their division. In a near rerun of the previous year, they disposed of the [Buccaneers](/wiki/2001_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2001 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") in a 31–9 game. In the second round, the Eagles defeated the [Bears](/wiki/2001_Chicago_Bears_season "2001 Chicago Bears season") 33–19 at [Soldier Field](/wiki/Soldier_Field "Soldier Field"). In the [NFC Championship game](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_St._Louis_Rams_29,_Philadelphia_Eagles_24 "2001–02 NFL playoffs"), they were unable to stop the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/2001_St._Louis_Rams_season "2001 St. Louis Rams season"), who defeated them 29–24.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

Despite injuries, McNabb led the Eagles to a 12–4 season in [2002](/wiki/2002_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2002 Philadelphia Eagles season").[[90]](#cite_note-90) Once again, they reached the [NFC Championship game](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_27,_Philadelphia_Eagles_10 "2002–03 NFL playoffs"), but lost at home 27–10 to the eventual Super Bowl champion [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2002_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2002 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") in the last game at [Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium").[[91]](#cite_note-i123-91)

In the opening game of the [2003 season](/wiki/2003_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2003 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles were shut out 17–0 by the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") in the first regular season game ever played at their new home, [Lincoln Financial Field](/wiki/Lincoln_Financial_Field "Lincoln Financial Field"). Once again, the team went 12–4 for the season and then reached the conference championship game. In doing so, the Eagles became the first team in modern history to get that far in the postseason after having been shut out at home in its first game. They achieved that distinction despite getting only five touchdown receptions all year by their wide receivers, which tied the league-low since the regular-season schedule was lengthened to its present 16 games in 1978 (this record would be broken in 2004 when the [New York Giants](/wiki/2004_New_York_Giants_season "2004 New York Giants season")' wide receivers caught only two touchdown passes). The Eagle receivers went through both September and October without a TD catch; the last time an NFL team had done that was in 1945. In the Divisional Round of the playoffs, the Eagles beat the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2003_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2003 Green Bay Packers season") 20–17 in overtime thanks in part to a late-game completion from McNabb to [Freddie Mitchell](/wiki/Freddie_Mitchell "Freddie Mitchell") on an iconic play that has come to be known as [4th and 26](/wiki/4th_and_26 "4th and 26"). They lost the [NFC Championship game](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Carolina_Panthers_14,_Philadelphia_Eagles_3 "2003–04 NFL playoffs") to the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2003_Carolina_Panthers_season "2003 Carolina Panthers season") 14–3. Panthers cornerback [Ricky Manning Jr.](/wiki/Ricky_Manning "Ricky Manning") had three interceptions in the game.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

The Eagles actively pursued premier wide receiver [Terrell Owens](/wiki/Terrell_Owens "Terrell Owens"), and acquired him in a controversial three-way deal with the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") and the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers"), on March 16, 2004.[[93]](#cite_note-93) The [2004 season](/wiki/2004_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2004 Philadelphia Eagles season") began with a bang as Owens caught three touchdown passes from McNabb in their season opener against the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants"). Owens ended up with exactly 1,200 receiving yards and 14 touchdown receptions, although his season ended prematurely with an ankle injury in a December 19 game against the Dallas Cowboys. The Eagles' 12–7 victory in this game gave them home-field advantage throughout the conference playoffs for the third year in a row. The Eagles tied a record by clinching the NFC East division crown (their fourth straight) after only their eleventh game of the season, matching the mark set by the [1985 Chicago Bears](/wiki/1985_Chicago_Bears_season "1985 Chicago Bears season") and the [1997 San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/1997_San_Francisco_49ers_season "1997 San Francisco 49ers season"). Their final two regular season games thus rendered meaningless, the Eagles sat out most of their first-string players in these games and lost both, yet still finished the season with a 13–3 record. McNabb had his best season to date, passing for 3,875 yards and 31 touchdowns, with only eight interceptions. This made him the first quarterback in NFL history to throw 30 or more TD passes and fewer than 10 interceptions in a single regular season. They then began their playoff run with the Divisional round at home against the sixth-seeded [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings"). The Eagles led from the start and never looked back, as McNabb led a very efficient passing attack (21 of 33 for 286 yards and 2 TDs), [Brian Westbrook](/wiki/Brian_Westbrook "Brian Westbrook") dominated on the ground with 70 rushing yards, and Freddie Mitchell performed very well on the receiving corps (5 receptions for 65 yards and a TD), as Philadelphia won 27–14, setting up their fourth-straight NFC Championship appearance. Facing the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons"), McNabb threw for 180 yards and two touchdowns, while also rushing for 32 yards. Westbrook rushed for 96 yards and caught five passes for 39. Winning the game 27–10, the Eagles advanced to [Super Bowl XXXIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIX "Super Bowl XXXIX"), where they faced the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2004_New_England_Patriots_season "2004 New England Patriots season"). Although McNabb threw 3 touchdown passes and 357 yards in the game, and the score was tied 14–14 going into the fourth quarter, the Patriots outscored the Eagles with ten straight points. McNabb completed a 30-yard touchdown pass, and the Eagles defense held the Patriots to a 3 and out, but a crucial interception with 46 seconds left sealed their fate, as the Patriots won 24–21.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

The team took a step back in [2005](/wiki/2005_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2005 Philadelphia Eagles season") with a 6–10 record, failing to make the playoffs for the first time since the 1999 season.[[95]](#cite_note-95) McNabb had played with a sports hernia and a broken thumb, starting 4–2 then losing three in a row, before he finally succumbed to injury and missed the rest of the season. For obnoxious behavior and a feud with McNabb, Owens was suspended after 7 games and was eventually cut.[[96]](#cite_note-q527-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

In [2006](/wiki/2006_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2006 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the team lost McNabb 10 games in and went into turmoil. However, Westbrook stepped up, and the Eagles earned their fifth NFC East title under coach Reid, with a 10–6 record. They won the Wild Card game against the New York Giants, but lost in the Divisional Round to the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints"). The [2007 season](/wiki/2007_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2007 Philadelphia Eagles season") would end with the Eagles finishing 8–8 and failing to make the playoffs for the second time in three seasons. However, in [2008](/wiki/2008_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2008 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the team finished the season with a 9–6–1 record, making the playoffs with a wild card berth, The team upset the Minnesota Vikings in the first round, winning the game 26–14. The team then went on to defeat the defending Super Bowl champion New York Giants 23–11 en route to their sixth [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/NFC_Championship_Game "NFC Championship Game"). In the [NFC Championship game](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Arizona_Cardinals_32,_Philadelphia_Eagles_25 "2008–09 NFL playoffs"), the Eagles rallied from down 24–6 at halftime to up 25–24 in the fourth quarter, but they lost to the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2008_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2008 Arizona Cardinals season") by a score of 32–25 after quarterback [Kurt Warner](/wiki/Kurt_Warner "Kurt Warner") scored a touchdown with just under three minutes remaining in regulation.[[98]](#cite_note-x410-98)

Entering the [2009 season](/wiki/2009_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2009 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles signed quarterback [Michael Vick](/wiki/Michael_Vick "Michael Vick").[[99]](#cite_note-99) On December 6, 2009, Andy Reid became only the fifth coach in NFL history to win 100 or more games with a single team in a single decade (the other four are [Tom Landry](/wiki/Tom_Landry "Tom Landry"), [Don Shula](/wiki/Don_Shula "Don Shula"), [Tony Dungy](/wiki/Tony_Dungy "Tony Dungy"), and [Bill Belichick](/wiki/Bill_Belichick "Bill Belichick")).[[100]](#cite_note-100) McNabb finally had a complete receiving corps, between first-round draft pick [Jeremy Maclin](/wiki/Jeremy_Maclin "Jeremy Maclin"), DeSean Jackson's 1,000-yard season, and [Brent Celek](/wiki/Brent_Celek "Brent Celek") ranking among the top 5 tight ends in the league. Without Brian Dawkins, defensive end [Trent Cole](/wiki/Trent_Cole "Trent Cole") stepped up and became the dominant force on defense with 12 sacks, earning him his second trip to the Pro Bowl and All-Pro honors. In 2009, the Eagles started 5–4, then won six straight. After a shutout by the Dallas Cowboys in week 17, the Eagles failed to secure a first round bye, and with a record of 11–5, they were the NFC's sixth seed. In their [January 2009 wild card game](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Dallas_Cowboys_34,_Philadelphia_Eagles_14 "2009–10 NFL playoffs"), the Eagles played against their divisional foes for the second consecutive week, losing 34–14 to hand Dallas their first playoff win since [December 1996](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Dallas_Cowboys_40,_Minnesota_Vikings_15 "1996–97 NFL playoffs").

On January 11, 2010, General Manager [Tom Heckert, Jr.](/wiki/Tom_Heckert,_Jr. "Tom Heckert, Jr.") was hired away by the Cleveland Browns; he was replaced by [Howie Roseman](/wiki/Howie_Roseman "Howie Roseman"), who was promoted from Vice President of Player Personnel.[[101]](#cite_note-101) On March 5, 2010, Brian Westbrook was cut from the Eagles after eight seasons with the team. On April 4, 2010, the team traded long-time starting quarterback Donovan McNabb to the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins") in exchange for a second-round draft pick.[[102]](#cite_note-102) [Kevin Kolb](/wiki/Kevin_Kolb "Kevin Kolb") was immediately named the starter for the [2010 season](/wiki/2010_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2010 Philadelphia Eagles season"), but after suffering a concussion in week 1 against the Packers, Vick took over as the starter. Week 4 saw the return of McNabb to Philadelphia. The Redskins got a touchdown early in the first quarter. After that, both offenses sputtered, and the Eagles had to settle for two field goals. But things rapidly fell apart when Vick injured his ribs and chest late in the first quarter when two Redskins defensive backs crushed him from both sides while running near the end zone. Kolb was once again brought out to play but delivered an uninspiring performance. He managed a touchdown pass in the fourth quarter, but it wasn't enough. A two-point conversion attempt after the touchdown failed, and Washington won 16–12. In Week 15, the Eagles beat the New York Giants in a stunning upset by overcoming a 21-point deficit in the second half. In the closing seconds of the game, [DeSean Jackson](/wiki/DeSean_Jackson "DeSean Jackson") returned a punt 65 yards for a touchdown to win 38–31. This became known as the [Miracle at the New Meadowlands](/wiki/Miracle_at_the_New_Meadowlands "Miracle at the New Meadowlands").[[103]](#cite_note-p407-103) Vick led the Eagles to their sixth NFC East division title in ten seasons.[[104]](#cite_note-104) With a record of 10–6, the Eagles clinched the third seed.[[105]](#cite_note-o518-105) In the [wild card round](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_NFL_playoffs "2010–11 NFL playoffs"), the Eagles lost 21–16 to the eventual [Super Bowl XLV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLV "Super Bowl XLV") champion Green Bay Packers.[[106]](#cite_note-l095-106)

The [2011 season](/wiki/2011_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2011 Philadelphia Eagles season") for the Eagles was a major disappointment. The offseason was marred by a lockout that began in March after the NFL's collective bargaining agreement expired, making practices, trades, and free agency impossible. During the draft, the Eagles did comparatively little. After the lockout ended in July, the team embarked on a rash of high-profile FA signings, including Raiders CB [Nnamdi Asomugha](/wiki/Nnamdi_Asomugha "Nnamdi Asomugha"), Dolphins RB [Ronnie Brown](/wiki/Ronnie_Brown "Ronnie Brown"), Giants WR [Steve Smith](/wiki/Steve_Smith_(wide_receiver,_born_1985) "Steve Smith (wide receiver, born 1985)"), Packers TE [Donald Lee](/wiki/Donald_Lee_(American_football) "Donald Lee (American football)"), Titans DE [Jason Babin](/wiki/Jason_Babin "Jason Babin"), and Packers DT [Cullen Jenkins](/wiki/Cullen_Jenkins "Cullen Jenkins"). Meanwhile, Kevin Kolb, displeased at losing the starting quarterback job to Michael Vick in 2010, was traded to Arizona for [Dominique Rodgers-Cromartie](/wiki/Dominique_Rodgers-Cromartie "Dominique Rodgers-Cromartie"). Replacing him as 2nd-stringer was ex-Titans quarterback [Vince Young](/wiki/Vince_Young "Vince Young"). Young created a lot of hype by calling Philadelphia the "Dream Team".[[107]](#cite_note-x988-107) The team managed to finish only 8–8 and missed the playoffs.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

In [2012](/wiki/2012_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2012 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles started off winning three of their first four games but lost their next eight, which eliminated them from the playoff hunt. They won only one of their last four games.[[109]](#cite_note-109) After a loss to the New York Giants on December 30, 2012, head coach Andy Reid was fired after fourteen seasons with the team.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

#### Chip Kelly years (2013–2015)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Fletcher_Cox_%2837014808491%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Fletcher_Cox_%2837014808491%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fletcher_Cox_(37014808491)_(cropped).jpg)

[Fletcher Cox](/wiki/Fletcher_Cox "Fletcher Cox"), Eagles defensive tackle from 2012 to 2023

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/WAS_at_PHI_Jan_2021_D50_3885_%2850805449387%29.jpg/250px-WAS_at_PHI_Jan_2021_D50_3885_%2850805449387%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:WAS_at_PHI_Jan_2021_D50_3885_(50805449387).jpg)

[Zach Ertz](/wiki/Zach_Ertz "Zach Ertz"), Eagles tight end from 2013 to 2021
