On January 16, 2013, after a 4–12 season, the Eagles brought in [University of Oregon](/wiki/University_of_Oregon "University of Oregon") head coach [Chip Kelly](/wiki/Chip_Kelly "Chip Kelly") to succeed Reid as head coach.[[111]](#cite_note-111) The Philadelphia Eagles named Michael Vick the starting quarterback going into the [2013 season](/wiki/2013_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2013 Philadelphia Eagles season") with much promise running Chip Kelly's [spread offense](/wiki/Spread_offense "Spread offense").[[112]](#cite_note-r901-112) The 2013 season proved very successful for the Eagles. While a hamstring injury took [Michael Vick](/wiki/Michael_Vick "Michael Vick") out after a 1–3 start, his backup [Nick Foles](/wiki/Nick_Foles "Nick Foles") led the team to a 10–6 regular-season record and its seventh NFC East title in 13 seasons, but the Eagles lost to the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2013_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2013 New Orleans Saints season") in the wild card round. Before throwing his first interception in Week 14, Foles had thrown 19 touchdowns, just one shy of the all-time NFL record of consecutive touchdowns without an interception to start a season, set earlier in the season by [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning"). Foles also tied Manning for most touchdown passes in a single game, with seven, against the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/2013_Oakland_Raiders_season "2013 Oakland Raiders season"), which also made him the youngest player in NFL history to throw for that many touchdowns in a game. Foles finished the regular season with 27 touchdown passes and only 2 interceptions, giving him the then-best TD-INT ratio in NFL history. (That record was later broken by [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady"), in the 2016 season.) He also finished with a 119.0 [passer rating](/wiki/Passer_rating "Passer rating"), third-highest in league history behind only [Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers") in 2011 and Peyton Manning in 2004. He was also only the second quarterback in NFL history to have a game in which he topped 400 passing yards and a perfect passer rating. [LeSean McCoy](/wiki/LeSean_McCoy "LeSean McCoy") finished his Pro Bowl season as the league's top rusher with 1,607 rushing yards (also a franchise record) and 2,146 total yards from scrimmage, also best in the NFL. As a whole, the Eagles offense scored 51 touchdowns, most in franchise history, passing the previous season-high set back in 1948. Following the 2013 season, the Eagles released Pro-Bowl wide receiver [DeSean Jackson](/wiki/DeSean_Jackson "DeSean Jackson") due to his poor "work ethic and attitude", as well as speculation of his involvement in gang-related activities.[[113]](#cite_note-113) The team signed All Pro safety [Malcolm Jenkins](/wiki/Malcolm_Jenkins "Malcolm Jenkins") to a three-year contract, worth $16.25 million.[[114]](#cite_note-j800-114)

The Eagles opened the [2014 season](/wiki/2014_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2014 Philadelphia Eagles season") winning their first three games and making NFL history as the only team ever to trail by ten or more points in their first three games and come back to win.[[115]](#cite_note-115) Nick Foles struggled with turnovers, but ultimately did well and led the Eagles to a 6–2 record, before breaking his collarbone, resulting in his replacement by [Mark Sanchez](/wiki/Mark_Sanchez "Mark Sanchez"), who outplayed Foles.[[116]](#cite_note-d730-116) The Eagles held the divisional title from Week 1 to Week 15. After going 9–3 with a crucial win over the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2014_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2014 Dallas Cowboys season"), the Eagles lost their next three, and a week after losing the NFC East title, they lost an upset against the 3–11 [Washington Redskins](/wiki/2014_Washington_Redskins_season "2014 Washington Redskins season") and were eliminated from playoff contention with the Cowboys' win over the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2014_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2014 Indianapolis Colts season").[[117]](#cite_note-l562-117)

Following the 2014 season, Chip Kelly was given total control and made some controversial personnel moves.[[118]](#cite_note-o561-118) He traded LeSean McCoy, who had become the team's all-time leading rusher after the 2014 season, for linebacker [Kiko Alonso](/wiki/Kiko_Alonso "Kiko Alonso"), a player Kelly coached at Oregon who had missed the entire 2014 season.[[119]](#cite_note-PhiladelphiaEagles.com-119) He also cut ten-year veteran and starter Trent Cole, who was still a consistent threat on defense and was second only to legend [Reggie White](/wiki/Reggie_White "Reggie White") on the Eagles all-time sack list.[[120]](#cite_note-120) He also traded the highly successful Nick Foles for [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford"), who had missed the entire 2014 season with a torn anterior cruciate ligament.[[121]](#cite_note-121) Kelly tried to re-sign Jeremy Maclin, who had stepped up as the team's leading wide receiver, but Maclin signed with the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") instead. However, the Eagles also acquired league leading rusher [DeMarco Murray](/wiki/DeMarco_Murray "DeMarco Murray"),[[122]](#cite_note-122) which not only helped the Eagles, but hurt their rivals, the Dallas Cowboys. They also obtained Super Bowl champion [Byron Maxwell](/wiki/Byron_Maxwell "Byron Maxwell"),[[123]](#cite_note-123) who left the Seattle Seahawks in free agency to sign a six-year, $63 million contract. The first two games of the [2015 season](/wiki/2015_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2015 Philadelphia Eagles season") were dismal, as they lost both. Bradford had a poor 2–4 TD-INT ratio, Maxwell was constantly beaten by [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2015_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2015 Atlanta Falcons season") wide receiver [Julio Jones](/wiki/Julio_Jones "Julio Jones"), and Murray was held to 11 yards on 21 carries. After Murray was injured, [Ryan Mathews](/wiki/Ryan_Mathews_(American_football) "Ryan Mathews (American football)") rushed for over 100 yards in a Week 3 win against the [New York Jets](/wiki/2015_New_York_Jets_season "2015 New York Jets season"). Kelly made Murray the unquestioned starter and although Murray's play improved over the season, he never regained his dominant form and was held to a career-low average of 3.6 yards per carry.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

On December 29, 2015, with one game left in the season, head coach Chip Kelly was released by the Eagles after a 6–9 record. Offensive coordinator [Pat Shurmur](/wiki/Pat_Shurmur "Pat Shurmur") was named interim head coach for the final game against the rival [New York Giants](/wiki/2015_New_York_Giants_season "2015 New York Giants season"), which Shurmur won 35–30.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

#### Doug Pederson years (2016–2020)

See also: [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII"), [Philly Special](/wiki/Philly_Special "Philly Special"), and [Double Doink](/wiki/Double_Doink "Double Doink")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Doug_Pederson_%28Eagles%29.jpg/250px-Doug_Pederson_%28Eagles%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Doug_Pederson_(Eagles).jpg)

[Doug Pederson](/wiki/Doug_Pederson "Doug Pederson"), Eagles head coach from 2016 to 2020, led the team to its first [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") win on February 4, 2018, in [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII").

The Eagles hired [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs")' offensive coordinator [Doug Pederson](/wiki/Doug_Pederson "Doug Pederson") as their next head coach on January 18, 2016.[[126]](#cite_note-u448-126) Pederson had been with the Chiefs for the preceding three years after having spent the four seasons before those with the Eagles. He served as a quality control assistant coach for the Eagles in 2009 and 2010 before being promoted to quarterbacks coach for the 2011 and 2012 seasons. He had been praised for his work with Chiefs quarterback [Alex Smith](/wiki/Alex_Smith "Alex Smith") over the prior several seasons, particularly 2015, as the Chiefs moved into the top 10 in scoring offense.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128)

At the end of the 2015 season, the Eagles were slated for the 13th overall pick in the [2016 NFL draft](/wiki/2016_NFL_draft "2016 NFL draft"). They traded that pick, [Byron Maxwell](/wiki/Byron_Maxwell "Byron Maxwell"), and [Kiko Alonso](/wiki/Kiko_Alonso "Kiko Alonso") to the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") for the eighth overall pick.[[129]](#cite_note-t641-129) Later, they traded the eighth overall pick, their third and fourth round picks, a 2017 first round pick, and a 2018 second round pick to the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") for the second overall pick and a 2017 fourth round pick.[[130]](#cite_note-f285-130) They used the second overall pick to draft [North Dakota State](/wiki/North_Dakota_State_Bison_football "North Dakota State Bison football") quarterback [Carson Wentz](/wiki/Carson_Wentz "Carson Wentz").[[131]](#cite_note-m634-131) On September 3, 2016, the Eagles traded starting quarterback [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford") to the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings"), who had lost [Teddy Bridgewater](/wiki/Teddy_Bridgewater "Teddy Bridgewater") for the season, for a 2017 first round pick and a 2018 fourth round pick.[[132]](#cite_note-m101-132) Following the trade, the Eagles named Wentz the starting quarterback for Week 1 of the [2016 season](/wiki/2016_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2016 Philadelphia Eagles season").[[133]](#cite_note-133)

Head coach Doug Pederson led the Eagles to a 3–0 record to start the season. His rookie quarterback started with five touchdowns, no interceptions and over 255 yards per game. After a Week 4 bye, they lost four out of the next five games, including losses to every team in their division. They also lost right tackle [Lane Johnson](/wiki/Lane_Johnson "Lane Johnson") to a 10-game suspension following the Week 5 loss against the Lions, which damaged Carson Wentz's hot start.[[134]](#cite_note-y588-134) In those four defeats, their average margin of loss was just under 5 points.[[135]](#cite_note-135) Pederson and the Eagles won just three of their final seven games. Although Wentz started off the season well, he finished with a TD–INT ratio of 8:7.[[136]](#cite_note-136) The rookie head coach-quarterback tandem led the Eagles to a 7–9 record, finishing last in the division.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

##### Super Bowl LII Champions

Main article: [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII")

See also: [Curse of Billy Penn](/wiki/Curse_of_Billy_Penn "Curse of Billy Penn") and [Philly Special](/wiki/Philly_Special "Philly Special")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Nick_Foles_2014_Pro_Bowl.jpg/250px-Nick_Foles_2014_Pro_Bowl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nick_Foles_2014_Pro_Bowl.jpg)

[Nick Foles](/wiki/Nick_Foles "Nick Foles"), Eagles quarterback from 2012 to 2014 and 2017 to 2018 and [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII")'s Most Valuable Player

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Jason_Kelce_Philadelphia_Eagles_Super_Bowl_LII_Victory_Parade_%2840140609012%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jason_Kelce_Philadelphia_Eagles_Super_Bowl_LII_Victory_Parade_%2840140609012%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jason_Kelce_Philadelphia_Eagles_Super_Bowl_LII_Victory_Parade_(40140609012)_(cropped).jpg)

[Jason Kelce](/wiki/Jason_Kelce "Jason Kelce"), Eagles center from 2011 to 2023, at the championship parade in Philadelphia on February 8, 2018

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Philadelphia_Eagles_Super_Bowl_LII_Victory_Parade_%2840140584832%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Philadelphia_Eagles_Super_Bowl_LII_Victory_Parade_%2840140584832%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Philadelphia_Eagles_Super_Bowl_LII_Victory_Parade_(40140584832)_(cropped).jpg)

[Malcolm Jenkins](/wiki/Malcolm_Jenkins "Malcolm Jenkins"), Eagles safety from 2014 to 2019, holds the [Vince Lombardi Trophy](/wiki/Vince_Lombardi_Trophy "Vince Lombardi Trophy") at the Eagles' February 2018 [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII") parade in [Center City Philadelphia](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia").

The Eagles started off the season with a 10–1 record and finished with a 13–3 record in [2017](/wiki/2017_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2017 Philadelphia Eagles season"), including a nine-game winning streak.[[138]](#cite_note-138) In a Week 14 game against the [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/2017_Los_Angeles_Rams_season "2017 Los Angeles Rams season"), starting quarterback [Carson Wentz](/wiki/Carson_Wentz "Carson Wentz") left the game with a torn anterior cruciate ligament, and backup [Nick Foles](/wiki/Nick_Foles "Nick Foles"), who had been re-signed in the off-season, took over for the rest of the season.[[139]](#cite_note-p416-139) Wentz was considered to have been playing at an MVP level at the time of his injury.[[140]](#cite_note-c796-140)[[141]](#cite_note-o027-141) Foles's first start was a comeback from a 20–7 deficit against the [New York Giants](/wiki/2017_New_York_Giants_season "2017 New York Giants season") where he scored four touchdowns to win 34–29.[[142]](#cite_note-142) Foles struggled in the last two games of the season against the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/2017_Oakland_Raiders_season "2017 Oakland Raiders season") and the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2017_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2017 Dallas Cowboys season") and threw a touchdown and two interceptions in those two games.[[143]](#cite_note-143) The Eagles clinched the #1 seed and home field advantage throughout the playoffs after the win against Oakland in Week 16.

Despite making history by being home underdogs, Foles led the Eagles past the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2017_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2017 Atlanta Falcons season") in the Divisional Round 15–10.[[144]](#cite_note-u816-144) In the [NFC Championship game](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Philadelphia_Eagles_38,_Minnesota_Vikings_7 "2017–18 NFL playoffs"), the Eagles dominated the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2017_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2017 Minnesota Vikings season") and their league best defense 38–7, again having been betting underdogs in the game.[[145]](#cite_note-g035-145) Foles elevated his play and threw for 352 passing yards and three touchdowns.[[146]](#cite_note-146) The Eagles traveled to [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis") to compete in [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII"), their third attempt at a title, against [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") and the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2017_New_England_Patriots_season "2017 New England Patriots season") in a rematch of [Super Bowl XXXIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIX "Super Bowl XXXIX") from 2005.[[147]](#cite_note-b194-147) In the second quarter, Philadelphia faced 4th and goal on the 1-yard line with 38 seconds left. Deciding to go for the touchdown, they attempted a trick play similar to one that had failed for the Patriots earlier. It would be the most memorable play of the game. As Foles stepped up to the running back position, Clement took a direct snap and pitched the ball to tight end [Trey Burton](/wiki/Trey_Burton "Trey Burton"), who then threw the ball to Foles, who was wide open in the right side of the end zone. Foles caught the ball, making him the first quarterback ever to catch a touchdown pass in a Super Bowl, and the ensuing extra point was good, giving the Eagles a 22–12 lead. The scoring play came to be known as the [Philly Special](/wiki/Philly_Special "Philly Special").[[148]](#cite_note-PBP-148) The Eagles went on to win 41–33, capturing their first Super Bowl [Vince Lombardi Trophy](/wiki/Vince_Lombardi_Trophy "Vince Lombardi Trophy") in franchise history and their first championship since 1960, ending the third-longest active championship drought in the NFL at 57 years.[[149]](#cite_note-h279-149) Foles won [Super Bowl MVP](/wiki/Super_Bowl_MVP "Super Bowl MVP") going 28 for 43 with 373 passing yards, three passing touchdowns, one interception, and one receiving touchdown.[[150]](#cite_note-e314-150) Foles became the first backup quarterback to start and win a Super Bowl since his opponent Tom Brady won as the backup for [Drew Bledsoe](/wiki/Drew_Bledsoe "Drew Bledsoe") in 2002's [Super Bowl XXXVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXVI "Super Bowl XXXVI").[[151]](#cite_note-e686-151)
The combined 74 points scored was one point shy of the Super Bowl record of 75, set in [Super Bowl XXIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXIX "Super Bowl XXIX") in 1995; this game marked only the second time in the history of the Super Bowl where the teams combined for 70+ points.[[152]](#cite_note-152) The game also set a record for most yardage by both teams (combined) with 1,151 yards, the most for any single game, regular season or postseason.[[153]](#cite_note-153)

Before the [2018 season](/wiki/2018_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2018 Philadelphia Eagles season") started, many injuries plagued the team, including quarterback Carson Wentz, who was still recovering from an anterior cruciate ligament injury that he had sustained during the previous season. Nick Foles was named the starting quarterback to begin the season,[[154]](#cite_note-154) and helped the team win their [opening game](/wiki/NFL_Kickoff "NFL Kickoff") against the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2018_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2018 Atlanta Falcons season"), 18–12. Wentz returned as the starting quarterback in week 3 after Foles had led the team to a 1–1 record.[[155]](#cite_note-155) Injuries continued to be a major problem throughout the season, especially on defense as [Jalen Mills](/wiki/Jalen_Mills "Jalen Mills"), [Ronald Darby](/wiki/Ronald_Darby "Ronald Darby"), and [Rodney McLeod](/wiki/Rodney_McLeod "Rodney McLeod") all suffered season-ending injuries.[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158) Wentz suffered a fractured vertebra in his back after a week 14 loss to the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2018_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2018 Dallas Cowboys season"),[[159]](#cite_note-159) and Foles was again named the starter for remainder of the season.[[160]](#cite_note-160) The Eagles' 4–6 record after 10 games seemed to give them little chance of making the playoffs, but the team managed to win 5 of their last 6, including upsets over the eventual NFC champion [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/2018_Los_Angeles_Rams_season "2018 Los Angeles Rams season") and [Houston Texans](/wiki/2018_Houston_Texans_season "2018 Houston Texans season"). The Eagles finished the season with a 9–7 record and made the playoffs as the sixth seed.[[161]](#cite_note-161)

In the [Wild Card](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Philadelphia_Eagles_16,_Chicago_Bears_15 "2018–19 NFL playoffs") game against the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2018_Chicago_Bears_season "2018 Chicago Bears season"), Foles threw 2 touchdowns and 2 interceptions in a 16–15 win. With ten seconds left, Bears kicker [Cody Parkey](/wiki/Cody_Parkey "Cody Parkey") missed a potential game-winning field goal that became known in NFL lore as the [Double Doink](/wiki/Double_Doink "Double Doink").[[162]](#cite_note-162) This sent the Philadelphia Eagles to an NFC Divisional matchup against the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2018_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2018 New Orleans Saints season"). The Eagles offense initially performed well, ending the first quarter with a 14–0 lead. The Eagles would not score again after the Saints defense rallied, forcing Foles to throw two interceptions. The Eagles ended up losing by a score of 20–14, ending their opportunity to repeat as Super Bowl champions[[163]](#cite_note-k805-163)

In [2019](/wiki/2019_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2019 Philadelphia Eagles season"), the Eagles matched their 9–7 record from 2018.[[164]](#cite_note-164) They won 4 straight games against divisional opponents to close the season, clinching the NFC East in Week 17 with a 34–17 win over the [New York Giants](/wiki/2019_New_York_Giants_season "2019 New York Giants season")[[165]](#cite_note-165) and clinched a playoff berth for the third consecutive season. Carson Wentz started his first playoff game in the [Wild Card](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Seattle_Seahawks_17,_Philadelphia_Eagles_9 "2019–20 NFL playoffs") round against the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2019_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2019 Seattle Seahawks season"), but left the game early in the first quarter after suffering a concussion on a controversial helmet-to-helmet hit by [Jadeveon Clowney](/wiki/Jadeveon_Clowney "Jadeveon Clowney").[[166]](#cite_note-166) Backup quarterback [Josh McCown](/wiki/Josh_McCown "Josh McCown") finished the game even after suffering a torn hamstring in the second quarter.[[167]](#cite_note-167) The Eagles lost to the Seahawks 17–9, ending their season.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

The Eagles opened the [2020 season](/wiki/2020_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2020 Philadelphia Eagles season") with consecutive losses to the [Washington Football Team](/wiki/2020_Washington_Football_Team_season "2020 Washington Football Team season") and [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/2020_Los_Angeles_Rams_season "2020 Los Angeles Rams season").[[169]](#cite_note-169)[[170]](#cite_note-170) In week 3, the Eagles [tied](/wiki/List_of_NFL_tied_games "List of NFL tied games") with the [Cincinnati Bengals](/wiki/2020_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2020 Cincinnati Bengals season") after controversially punting the ball instead of attempting a potential 64-yard game-winning field goal late in overtime.[[171]](#cite_note-171) The decision was widely criticized by sports media and fans as they accused head coach Doug Pederson of settling for a tie, rather than playing to win.[[172]](#cite_note-172) Once again, injuries continued to plague the team as almost every offensive starter had suffered an injury throughout the season,[[173]](#cite_note-173) and the team fielded 14 different offensive line combinations in 16 games.[[174]](#cite_note-174) The team entered the bye week with a 3–4–1 record and proceeded to lose their next four games. During their week 13 match up against the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2020_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2020 Green Bay Packers season"), rookie quarterback [Jalen Hurts](/wiki/Jalen_Hurts "Jalen Hurts") entered the game in the third quarter in relief of Wentz after poor play.[[175]](#cite_note-175) Hurts would later be named the starting quarterback for the remainder of the season after a career-worst season performance by Wentz.[[176]](#cite_note-176)[[177]](#cite_note-177) Hurts led the Eagles to a 24–21 victory against the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2020_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2020 New Orleans Saints season") in week 14. This would be the team's last win of the 2020 season as they lost their next three games, finishing with a 4–11–1 record and missing the playoffs for the first time in four seasons.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

During the off-season, defensive coordinator [Jim Schwartz](/wiki/Jim_Schwartz "Jim Schwartz") announced that he would step down from his position.[[179]](#cite_note-179) A day later, linebackers coach [Ken Flajole](/wiki/Ken_Flajole "Ken Flajole") left his position on the team.[[180]](#cite_note-180) Doug Pederson met with Eagles owner Jeffrey Lurie to discuss the future of the team. Pederson proposed changes to the coaching staff that were described by internal reporters as "underwhelming" and out of line with Lurie's vision for the team.[[181]](#cite_note-181)[[182]](#cite_note-182) On January 11, 2021, the Eagles announced that they had fired Pederson.[[183]](#cite_note-183) In a statement, Lurie described the move as in the best interests of both Pederson and the team.[[184]](#cite_note-184) Pederson became just the first head coach to be fired within three years of winning a Super Bowl since the [Baltimore Colts](/wiki/Baltimore_Colts "Baltimore Colts") fired [Don McCafferty](/wiki/Don_McCafferty "Don McCafferty") after the [1972 NFL season](/wiki/1972_NFL_season "1972 NFL season").[[185]](#cite_note-185)

#### Nick Sirianni years (2021–present)

See also: [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII") and [Super Bowl LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Jalen_Hurts_11-14-22_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jalen_Hurts_11-14-22_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jalen_Hurts_11-14-22_(cropped).jpg)

[Jalen Hurts](/wiki/Jalen_Hurts "Jalen Hurts"), Eagles quarterback since 2020

The Eagles hired former [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts")' offensive coordinator [Nick Sirianni](/wiki/Nick_Sirianni "Nick Sirianni") as their next head coach on January 21, 2021.[[186]](#cite_note-s452-186) During the offseason, the Eagles traded starting quarterback [Carson Wentz](/wiki/Carson_Wentz "Carson Wentz") to the Colts for a 2021 third round draft pick and a 2022 conditional second round pick.[[187]](#cite_note-e988-187) In doing so, Wentz was reunited with [Frank Reich](/wiki/Frank_Reich "Frank Reich"), head coach of the Colts who served as the Eagles' offensive coordinator for Wentz's first two years in Philadelphia, including during their [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII")-winning season in 2017. The Eagles absorbed a $33.8-million dead cap hit for trading Wentz.[[188]](#cite_note-s857-188)
During the [2021 NFL draft](/wiki/2021_NFL_draft "2021 NFL draft"), the Eagles selected [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy")–winning wide receiver [DeVonta Smith](/wiki/DeVonta_Smith "DeVonta Smith") with the 10th overall pick,[[189]](#cite_note-189) and maneuvered around the draft to gain an extra first-round selection in the [2022 NFL draft](/wiki/2022_NFL_draft "2022 NFL draft").[[190]](#cite_note-s382-190) Smith was a highly touted prospect out of the [University of Alabama](/wiki/University_of_Alabama "University of Alabama"), and was the first Heisman winner the Eagles drafted in over fifty years.[[191]](#cite_note-191) The drafting of Smith also reunited him with [Jalen Hurts](/wiki/Jalen_Hurts "Jalen Hurts"), his first quarterback at Alabama.[[192]](#cite_note-u727-192) After training camp, Hurts was officially named the Eagles' starting quarterback for the [2021 season](/wiki/2021_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2021 Philadelphia Eagles season") by head coach Sirianni.[[193]](#cite_note-z618-193) After starting the season 2–5, the Eagles went 7–3 in their last ten games, clinching a playoff berth in week 17 after a victory over the [Washington Football Team](/wiki/2021_Washington_Football_Team_season "2021 Washington Football Team season") along with a loss by the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2021_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2021 Minnesota Vikings season") and win by the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2021_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2021 San Francisco 49ers season").[[194]](#cite_note-194) The Eagles finished the season with the #1 rushing offense; It was the best rushing offense since the [1985 Chicago Bears](/wiki/1985_Chicago_Bears_season "1985 Chicago Bears season"), and the best in the franchise history since the [1949 Eagles team](/wiki/1949_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1949 Philadelphia Eagles season"). They lost in the wild card game against the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2021_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2021 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") 31–15.[[195]](#cite_note-195)

During the [2022 NFL draft](/wiki/2022_NFL_draft "2022 NFL draft"), the Eagles traded the 18th overall pick to the [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/Tennessee_Titans "Tennessee Titans") for pro bowl wide receiver [AJ Brown](/wiki/AJ_Brown "AJ Brown").[[196]](#cite_note-196) With the addition and pairing of Brown with [Devonta Smith](/wiki/Devonta_Smith "Devonta Smith"), the team saw an elevation of play from second year quarterback [Jalen Hurts](/wiki/Jalen_Hurts "Jalen Hurts"). The Eagles went on to win their first eight games of the [2022 season](/wiki/2022_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2022 Philadelphia Eagles season"), starting 8–0 for the first time in franchise history.[[197]](#cite_note-197) After a 25–20 road win over the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2022_Chicago_Bears_season "2022 Chicago Bears season") in Week 15, the Eagles matched their franchise-best 13–1 start during the [2004 season](/wiki/2004_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2004 Philadelphia Eagles season") and secured their third 13-win season in franchise history and first since [2017](/wiki/2017_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2017 Philadelphia Eagles season"). In Week 18, after losses in the previous two games, Hurts returned from a late season shoulder injury and helped the Eagles to a 22–16 win over the Giants, clinching the NFC East for the first time since [2019](/wiki/2019_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2019 Philadelphia Eagles season"), and the NFC's #1 seed for the first time since [2017](/wiki/2017_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2017 Philadelphia Eagles season").[[198]](#cite_note-r917-198) The Eagles also reached 14 regular season wins, a franchise record.[[199]](#cite_note-l184-199) Hurts was named as one of the five finalists for the [NFL Most Valuable Player](/wiki/NFL_Most_Valuable_Player "NFL Most Valuable Player") Award, where he would eventually finish second in voting.[[200]](#cite_note-200)[[201]](#cite_note-201)

In the Divisional Round, the Eagles routed the Giants 38–7 for their first postseason win since [2018](/wiki/2018_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2018 Philadelphia Eagles season").[[202]](#cite_note-c692-202) With this win, Philadelphia completed a 3–0 sweep of all three games played against New York, and advanced to the NFC Championship for the first time since 2017. The Eagles went on to win the NFC Championship against the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2022_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2022 San Francisco 49ers season"), who finished the game injury depleted, 31–7, advancing to [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII").[[203]](#cite_note-k447-203) In their fourth Super Bowl appearance in franchise history, the Eagles faced the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2022_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2022 Kansas City Chiefs season") and former longtime head coach [Andy Reid](/wiki/Andy_Reid "Andy Reid"). In the game, Hurts' 70 rushing yards three rushing touchdowns broke the record for the most rushing yards and rushing touchdowns by a quarterback in Super Bowl history,[[204]](#cite_note-204)[[205]](#cite_note-205) despite this and holding a 24–14 lead at halftime, the Eagles ultimately lost on a Chiefs late field goal and a final score of 38–35.[[206]](#cite_note-z880-206)

The Eagles opened the 2023 season as the defending NFC Champions. Despite starting the season 10–1, The Eagles failed to equal or improve on their 14–3 record from the previous year. Even in victory, the Eagles had problems with a poor defense the entire season, which was ranked 26th out of 32;[[207]](#cite_note-207) 8 of their 11 victories fell within one score, and their losses to the 49ers and Cowboys were blowouts. The Eagles' slump continued into the [playoffs](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_NFL_playoffs "2023–24 NFL playoffs"), in which they were eliminated in the Wild Card round by the [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South") champion [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2023_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2023 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") in a 32–9 blowout loss. The Eagles promptly fired offensive coordinator [Brian Johnson](/wiki/Brian_Johnson_(American_football_coach) "Brian Johnson (American football coach)") and defensive coordinator [Sean Desai](/wiki/Sean_Desai "Sean Desai") within a week of their playoff exit.[[208]](#cite_note-208)[[209]](#cite_note-209)

In the subsequent offseason, on March 4, 2024, center [Jason Kelce](/wiki/Jason_Kelce "Jason Kelce") retired after spending his entire 13-year career with the Eagles.[[210]](#cite_note-l150-210) On March 10, defensive tackle [Fletcher Cox](/wiki/Fletcher_Cox "Fletcher Cox") would also retire after spending 12 seasons with the team.[[211]](#cite_note-y166-211) The Eagles hired veteran defensive coach [Vic Fangio](/wiki/Vic_Fangio "Vic Fangio"), who had served as a consultant for the team during the 2022 season, as their new defensive coordinator.[[212]](#cite_note-212) Former [Chargers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Chargers "Los Angeles Chargers") offensive coordinator [Kellen Moore](/wiki/Kellen_Moore "Kellen Moore") was brought in for the offense.[[213]](#cite_note-213)

##### Super Bowl LIX Champions

Main article: [Super Bowl LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX")

On March 13, 2024, [Saquon Barkley](/wiki/Saquon_Barkley "Saquon Barkley"), a former running back with the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants"), became a free agent, and signed a three-year $37.75 million deal with the Eagles. On September 6, 2024, the Eagles opened their season in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), Brazil. In his debut, Barkley rushed for 109 yards with two rushing touchdowns and a receiving touchdown in a 34–29 victory over the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers"), becoming the first Eagles player to score three touchdowns in their debut with the team since [Terrell Owens](/wiki/Terrell_Owens "Terrell Owens") in [2004](/wiki/2004_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2004 Philadelphia Eagles season").[[214]](#cite_note-s535-214) Despite a turbulent 2–2 start to the season, the Eagles improved on their 11–6 record from the previous season, and won ten consecutive games for the first time in franchise history. For the fourth straight year and seventh time in the last 8 seasons the Eagles clinched a playoff berth. Barkley continued his dominance in Week 12 against the [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams"), recording a franchise record 255 rushing yards and 302 total yards.[[215]](#cite_note-215)[[216]](#cite_note-216)[[217]](#cite_note-217) In Week 17, the Eagles clinched the [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") by sweeping the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2024_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2024 Dallas Cowboys season") for the first time since [2011](/wiki/2011_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2011 Philadelphia Eagles season"), with Barkley running for 167 yards and crossing the [2,000 yard](/wiki/2,000-yard_club "2,000-yard club") mark, becoming only the ninth running back in NFL history to do so.[[218]](#cite_note-218) With their Week 18 win over the [New York Giants](/wiki/2024_New_York_Giants_season "2024 New York Giants season"), the Eagles finished 14–3 for the second time in three seasons.

The Eagles started the playoffs by defeating the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2024_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2024 Green Bay Packers season") 22–10 in the Wild Card Round. In a snowy rematch of their Week 12 meeting, the Eagles again beat the [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/2024_Los_Angeles_Rams_season "2024 Los Angeles Rams season") in the Divisional Round 28–22. In the [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/2024_NFC_Championship_Game "2024 NFC Championship Game"), the Eagles dominated their division rival [Washington Commanders](/wiki/2024_Washington_Commanders_season "2024 Washington Commanders season") 55–23, setting the record for the most points scored in a conference championship game.[[219]](#cite_note-219) In [Super Bowl LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX"), the Eagles met the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2024_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2024 Kansas City Chiefs season") in a rematch of [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII") from two years earlier, this time defeating the two-time defending champions by a score of 40–22 and winning their second Super Bowl and first since the [2017](/wiki/2017_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2017 Philadelphia Eagles season") season.[[220]](#cite_note-a252-220) The Eagles' win prevented the Chiefs from achieving the first-ever Super Bowl [three-peat](/wiki/Three-peat "Three-peat"). Barkley recorded 57 rushing yards, setting the NFL record for most rushing (2,504) and scrimmage yards (2,857) in a full season surpassing [Terrell Davis](/wiki/Terrell_Davis "Terrell Davis").[[221]](#cite_note-221) Philadelphia quarterback [Jalen Hurts](/wiki/Jalen_Hurts "Jalen Hurts") threw for 221 yards with two touchdowns and rushed for 72 yards and a touchdown, breaking his own record for most rushing yards for a quarterback in a Super Bowl, with 72.[[222]](#cite_note-222)
Hurts was named [Super Bowl MVP](/wiki/Super_Bowl_MVP "Super Bowl MVP") for his performance in the victory.[[223]](#cite_note-223) The Eagles finished with 18 total wins, tying the NFL record for most total wins in a season along with the [1984 San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/1984_San_Francisco_49ers_season "1984 San Francisco 49ers season"), [1985 Chicago Bears](/wiki/1985_Chicago_Bears_season "1985 Chicago Bears season"), and [2007 New England Patriots](/wiki/2007_New_England_Patriots_season "2007 New England Patriots season"). The Eagles 145 points scored set an NFL record for the most points scored in a postseason.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

2025 was the Eagles' fourth consecutive season with a new offensive coordinator, as [Kellen Moore](/wiki/Kellen_Moore "Kellen Moore") was hired as the head coach of the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") following the Super Bowl. The Eagles opened their season against the rival [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys"), raising their second World Championship banner en route to a 24-20 victory. After initially retiring in the offseason, longest tenured Eagle [Brandon Graham](/wiki/Brandon_Graham "Brandon Graham") finalized a contract to return to the Eagles after the teams Week 9 bye week.[[225]](#cite_note-225) The Eagles were not able to improve on their 14–3 record from the previous season after a second-half comeback loss to the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") in Week 12, and failed to match it after an upset loss to the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears") the following week. With their Week 15 shutout win over the [Las Vegas Raiders](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Raiders "Las Vegas Raiders"), the Eagles secured their fifth consecutive winning season with [Brandon Graham](/wiki/Brandon_Graham "Brandon Graham") recording two sacks, becoming the oldest player in Eagles history to record a sack and passing hall of famer [Richard Dent](/wiki/Richard_Dent "Richard Dent").[[226]](#cite_note-226) After their Week 16 win over the division rival [Washington Commanders](/wiki/Washington_Commanders "Washington Commanders"), the Eagles clinched the NFC East division title, becoming the first team to repeat as NFC East champions since the [2004 Eagles](/wiki/2004_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2004 Philadelphia Eagles season"),[[227]](#cite_note-227) and becoming the only 2024 division winner to win their division again in 2025. In the playoffs, the Eagles offensive inconsistencies caught up to them, as they were defeated in the Wild Card round by the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2025_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2025 San Francisco 49ers season"), 23–19, ending their hopes for a repeat.[[228]](#cite_note-228) Immediately following on January 13, 2026, [Patullo](/wiki/Kevin_Patullo "Kevin Patullo") and Philadelphia parted ways,[[229]](#cite_note-229) with pundits and players calling Patullo's playcalling and offense uninspiring and very predictable.[[230]](#cite_note-230)[[231]](#cite_note-231)

On January 29, 2026, the Eagles hired new coordinator [Sean Mannion](/wiki/Sean_Mannion_(American_football) "Sean Mannion (American football)") as part of their offensive coaching overhaul.[[232]](#cite_note-232) Long time and highly regarded offensive line coach [Jeff Stoutland](/wiki/Jeff_Stoutland "Jeff Stoutland") also stepped away from the team after 13 seasons.[[233]](#cite_note-233)[[234]](#cite_note-234)

### Division championships

The Eagles were a part of the NFL Eastern Division from 1933 to 1949, the National Conference from 1950 to 1953, and the Eastern Conference from 1953 to 1966. They were then placed in the Capitol Division of the Eastern Conference in 1967. When the league reformed into the NFC and AFC in 1970, the Eagles were placed in the NFC East.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Philadelphia_Eagles_wordmark_%282022%E2%80%93present%29.svg/250px-Philadelphia_Eagles_wordmark_%282022%E2%80%93present%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Philadelphia_Eagles_wordmark_(2022%E2%80%93present).svg)

The choice of an eagle as the team mascot honored the insignia of the [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") program, the [National Recovery Administration](/wiki/National_Recovery_Administration "National Recovery Administration"), which featured a blue eagle as its insignia.[[235]](#cite_note-235)[[note 1]](#cite_note-236)

For the 1933 and 1934 seasons, the Eagles colors were light blue and yellow.[[236]](#cite_note-237) In 1935, they added green to their uniforms and for several decades, their [colors](/wiki/Away_colours "Away colours") were [kelly green](/wiki/Kelly_green "Kelly green"), silver, and white. In 1954 the Eagles, along with the [Baltimore Colts](/wiki/Baltimore_Colts "Baltimore Colts"), became the second team ever in the NFL to put a [logo](/wiki/Logo#Sports "Logo") on their [helmets](/wiki/Football_helmet "Football helmet"), with silver wings on a kelly green helmet. In 1969 the team wore two helmet versions: Kelly green with white wings in road games, and white with kelly green wings at home. From 1970 to '73, they wore the white helmets with Kelly green wings exclusively before switching back to Kelly green helmets with silver wings. By 1974, Joseph A. Scirrotto Jr. designed the silver wings within a white outline, and this style on a kelly green helmet became standard for over two decades.

From 1948 to 1995, the team logo was an eagle in flight carrying a football in its claws, although from 1969 to 1972, the eagle took on a more stylized look.

In 1973, the team's name was added below the eagle, returning to its pre-1969 look. Both the logo and uniforms were radically altered in 1996, when the primary kelly green color was changed to a darker shade, officially described as "midnight green". Silver was practically abandoned, as uniform pants moved to either white or midnight green. The traditional helmet wings were changed to a primarily white color, with silver and black accents. The team's logo combination (the eagle and club name lettering) also changed, with the eagle itself limited to a white (bald eagle) head, drawn in a less realistic, more cartoon-like style, and the lettering changed from calligraphic to block letters.[[237]](#cite_note-238) The eagle head is the only NFL logo to "face left" which causes the right side of the logo to present a stylized "E".[[238]](#cite_note-239)

Since 1996, the team has made only minor alterations, mostly related to jersey/pants combinations worn during specific games. For example, in 1997, against the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/1997_San_Francisco_49ers_season "1997 San Francisco 49ers season"), the team wore midnight green jerseys and pants for the first of only two occasions in team history. The second occasion was in 2002, during the final regular season game at [Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium"), a win over the division rival [Washington Redskins](/wiki/2002_Washington_Redskins_season "2002 Washington Redskins season"). A year later, in the first two games of the 2003 season (both home losses, to the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2003_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2003 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") and [New England Patriots](/wiki/2003_New_England_Patriots_season "2003 New England Patriots season")), the Eagles wore white jerseys with white pants. After that, the white jerseys along with white pants were worn exclusively during preseason games from 2004 to 2011, with the exception of 2007 and 2010.

The 2003 season also saw the first (though subtle) change to the 1996-style uniform. On both white and green jerseys, black shadows and silver trim were added to both the green and white numbering. The stripe on the pants changed from black-green-black to black-silver-green on the white pants, and from a solid black stripe to one stripe of black, another of silver, with one small white stripe in between for the midnight green pants. The 2003 season also saw the team debut black alternate jerseys, with a green (instead of black) shadow on white numbers, and silver trim. These black jerseys have been worn for two selected [home](/wiki/Home_(sports) "Home (sports)") games each season (usually the first home game after a [bye](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)") week and the season finale). In the 2003 and 2004 regular season home finales, the team wore the green [road](/wiki/Road_(sports) "Road (sports)") pants with the black alternate jerseys, but lost both games. Since then, the Eagles have only worn the black jerseys with the white pants. However, due to the special 75th anniversary uniforms serving as the "alternates" for one game in 2007, the Eagles could not wear the alternate black jersey that season per league rules at the time (alternate uniforms were permitted twice per season but only one can be used). The black jerseys with white pants, however, reappeared for the 2008 [Thanksgiving](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") night game against the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2008_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2008 Arizona Cardinals season"). From 2006 to 2013, the Eagles have only worn the alternate black jerseys once a season and for the last November home game, but did not use them in 2007, 2010, and 2011. For the 2007 and 2010 seasons, the Eagles used throwback uniforms in place of the black alternates for their anniversary to commemorate past teams. The team also started wearing black cleats exclusively in 2004, although the rule was relaxed by the mid-2010s.

To celebrate the team's 75th anniversary, the 2007 uniforms featured a 75th season logo patch on the left shoulder. In addition, the team wore ["throwback" jerseys](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") in a 2007 game against the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2007_Detroit_Lions_season "2007 Detroit Lions season"). The yellow and blue jerseys, the same colors found on [Philadelphia's city flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Philadelphia "Flag of Philadelphia"), are based on those worn by the Philadelphia Eagles in the team's inaugural season, and had been the same colors used by the [Frankford Yellow Jackets](/wiki/Frankford_Yellow_Jackets "Frankford Yellow Jackets") franchise prior to its suspension of operations in 1931. The Eagles beat Detroit, 56–21.[[239]](#cite_note-240)

In the 2010 season against the Green Bay Packers, on September 12, 2010, the Eagles wore uniforms similar to the ones that were worn by the 1960 championship team in honor the 50th anniversary of that team.[[240]](#cite_note-241) In weeks 4 and 6 of the 2010 season, the Eagles wore their white jerseys in a matchup against the Washington Redskins and Atlanta Falcons, respectively, before reverting to their midnight green jerseys for the rest of their home games.

For the 2012 season, [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") took over from [Reebok](/wiki/Reebok "Reebok") as the NFL's official apparel licensee, but the Eagles decided that they would not be adopting Nike's "Elite 51" uniform technology. Aside from the Nike logo replacing the Reebok logo, the only other change is the league-wide revision of the NFL shield on the uniform (replacing the NFL Equipment logo). Other than that the uniforms essentially remain unchanged. The Eagles also revived their black alternate jersey and resumed wearing white pants with their white jerseys in the regular season.

For the 2014 season, the Eagles officially adopted the "Elite 51"-style uniform from Nike. However, they only wore the midnight green jerseys and pants in the second half of that season due to the difficulty of producing their preferred shade of midnight green.[[241]](#cite_note-t103-242)

In Week 6 of 2014 against the New York Giants, the team introduced black pants to complement their black jerseys, giving them a blackout uniform set. The Eagles won the game 27–0. The victory was their first shutout in 18 years. In Week 16 of 2016 (also against the Giants), the Eagles wore a variation of the all-black look as part of the [NFL Color Rush](/wiki/NFL_Color_Rush "NFL Color Rush") program, but with solid black socks as opposed to black with white sanitary socks of the original look. With the exception of 2017 and 2025, the Eagles have regularly worn the all-black uniforms at home against the Giants since the 2014 'blackout' game.[[242]](#cite_note-243)[[243]](#cite_note-244) The black jerseys/white pants combination was last seen during a preseason road game against the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2014_New_England_Patriots_season "2014 New England Patriots season"), after which the black jerseys were paired exclusively with the black pants.

In 2018, the Eagles' midnight green pants were not worn at all, marking the first such instance since the 1996 rebrand that the Eagles wore only white pants with their primary white or midnight green jerseys.

In Week 6 of the 2017 season, the Eagles debuted an all-white look with white jerseys, white pants and solid white socks in a road game against the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2017_Carolina_Panthers_season "2017 Carolina Panthers season").[[244]](#cite_note-v091-245) The all-white look began to be utilized full Time as a road uniform set in 2019 thanks to the NFL's decision to allow teams to wear solid color socks as an alternate look.

During Week 10 of the 2021 season, the Eagles paired their white uniforms with the alternate black pants against the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2021_Denver_Broncos_season "2021 Denver Broncos season"), marking the first time the black pants were paired with a different colored uniform.

In 2022, the Eagles unveiled a new black alternate helmet, which would be worn alongside the all-black uniform. In addition, the team announced that throwback Kelly Green uniforms would return as an alternate uniform starting in the 2023 season.[[245]](#cite_note-e555-246) The team originally intended to release the uniforms in 2022, but due to production supply issues, the Eagles elected to delay its release to the 2023 season. The Eagles returned to wearing Kelly Green uniforms as the teams alternate, with their debut being in a Week 7 win vs the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2023_Miami_Dolphins_season "2023 Miami Dolphins season"). With the Kelly Green helmet becoming the team's one alternate, the black helmet was temporarily shelved, and the Eagles returned to wearing their primary midnight green helmets with the all-black uniform.

Back in 2022, it was released to the media that the Eagles would be changing their wordmark to a more modernized design.[[246]](#cite_note-z656-247) Due to supply chain issues, the new wordmark was not able to be applied to the uniforms until the 2024 season. In addition, the black helmet was brought back as the team's third helmet option; while the NFL only allowed teams with new designs to adopt a third helmet in 2024, the Eagles' minor change of jersey wordmark counted as such. The black helmets were worn with the white jerseys, black pants and white socks for the team's Week 1 game in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), Brazil against the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2024_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2024 Green Bay Packers season"), and again in a 2025 Week 10 rematch at [Lambeau Field](/wiki/Lambeau_Field "Lambeau Field").

## Rivalries

### Divisional

#### Washington Commanders

Main article: [Commanders–Eagles rivalry](/wiki/Commanders%E2%80%93Eagles_rivalry "Commanders–Eagles rivalry")

See also: [Body Bag Game](/wiki/Body_Bag_Game "Body Bag Game")

While not as big as the rivalries with the Giants and Cowboys, the Eagles' historical rivalry with the [Washington Redskins / Washington Football Team / Washington Commanders](/wiki/Washington_Commanders "Washington Commanders") has still been fierce. Although the two franchises played in the same division in the Eagles' inaugural 1933 season, their first meeting did not take place until October 21, 1934, during Washington's first year under the Boston Redskins moniker; the Redskins defeated the Eagles 6–0 at [Fenway Park](/wiki/Fenway_Park "Fenway Park").[[247]](#cite_note-248) Washington currently leads the all-time series 90–87–6. Since 2010, the rivalry has been very even overall with the Eagles winning 19 of the last 31 matchups.[[248]](#cite_note-249)

#### Dallas Cowboys

Main article: [Cowboys–Eagles rivalry](/wiki/Cowboys%E2%80%93Eagles_rivalry "Cowboys–Eagles rivalry")

See also: [Bounty Bowl](/wiki/Bounty_Bowl "Bounty Bowl")

The Eagles and [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") rivalry has been listed among the best and most acrimonious in the NFL.[[249]](#cite_note-250)[[250]](#cite_note-251) The Eagles won the first game in this rivalry 27–25 on September 30, 1960. Dallas leads the all-time series 74–58 as of the 2024 season, but in recent years, the series has been close, with each team winning 12 games since 2006.[[251]](#cite_note-252) There is considerable hostility between the two teams' fan bases, with incidents such as the 1989 [Bounty Bowl](/wiki/Bounty_Bowl "Bounty Bowl"). The rivalry has even spilled over into [draft weekend](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft"), with Cowboys legend [Drew Pearson](/wiki/Drew_Pearson_(American_football) "Drew Pearson (American football)") and Eagles legend [David Akers](/wiki/David_Akers "David Akers") exchanging insults at the opposing franchise in [2017](/wiki/2017_NFL_draft "2017 NFL draft") and [2018](/wiki/2018_NFL_draft "2018 NFL draft"), respectively.[[252]](#cite_note-253)[[253]](#cite_note-254)

#### New York Giants

Main article: [Eagles–Giants rivalry](/wiki/Eagles%E2%80%93Giants_rivalry "Eagles–Giants rivalry")

The Eagles' rivalry with the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") began in 1933 with the founding of the Eagles and slowly strengthened when both teams came to relative prominence in the 1940s and 1950s. The two teams have played in the same division in the NFL every year since 1933. The ferocity of the rivalry can also be attributed to the geographic [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")–[Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") rivalry, which is mirrored in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")'s [Mets–Phillies](/wiki/Mets%E2%80%93Phillies_rivalry "Mets–Phillies rivalry") and the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")'s [Flyers–Rangers](/wiki/Flyers%E2%80%93Rangers_rivalry "Flyers–Rangers rivalry") and [Flyers–Islanders](/wiki/Flyers%E2%80%93Islanders_rivalry "Flyers–Islanders rivalry"). It is ranked by [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network") as one of the greatest rivalries of all time,[[254]](#cite_note-255) *Sports Illustrated* ranks it as the fourth best NFL rivalry of all time,[[49]](#cite_note-Giants-Eagles_rivalry-49) and according to [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN"), it is one of the fiercest and most well-known rivalries in the football community.[[255]](#cite_note-256) As of the 2024 season, the Eagles lead the all-time series 96–89–2.[[256]](#cite_note-257)

### Conference

#### Atlanta Falcons

The Eagles lead the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons") 21–16–1, with a 3–1 lead in playoff games.[[257]](#cite_note-258) The rivalry first emerged after the Falcons upset the Eagles in the [1978 Wild Card Round](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Atlanta_Falcons_14,_Philadelphia_Eagles_13 "1978–79 NFL playoffs") 14–13,[[258]](#cite_note-259) and only intensified further during the 2000s thanks to the rivalry between prominent [dual-threat quarterbacks](/wiki/Dual-threat_quarterback "Dual-threat quarterback") [Donovan McNabb](/wiki/Donovan_McNabb "Donovan McNabb") and [Michael Vick](/wiki/Michael_Vick "Michael Vick"). Recently, the Eagles' path to winning [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII") included a [2017 divisional round](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Philadelphia_Eagles_15,_Atlanta_Falcons_10 "2017–18 NFL playoffs") victory over the Falcons.[[259]](#cite_note-260)[[260]](#cite_note-261)

#### Tampa Bay Buccaneers

Main article: [Buccaneers–Eagles rivalry](/w/index.php?title=Buccaneers%E2%80%93Eagles_rivalry&action=edit&redlink=1 "Buccaneers–Eagles rivalry (page does not exist)")

The Eagles trail the overall series 13-12, with having a 2-4 record against the Buccaneers in the playoffs. The most notable matchup was the [2002 NFC Championship Game](/wiki/2002-03_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_27,_Philadelphia_Eagles_10 "2002-03 NFL playoffs"), where the Eagles lost 27-10, and was also the final game ever played at [Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium").

### Interconference

#### Kansas City Chiefs

Despite playing in separate conferences, the Eagles played the Chiefs at least once a year since 2021, with two of those match ups being [Super Bowls](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"). The Chiefs beat the Eagles in [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII") 38-35, with the Eagles getting their revenge in [Super Bowl LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX"), winning 40-22.

#### Pittsburgh Steelers

Main article: [Eagles–Steelers rivalry](/wiki/Eagles%E2%80%93Steelers_rivalry "Eagles–Steelers rivalry")

The Eagles and [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") are both located in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") and began play in 1933.[[261]](#cite_note-262) From that season through 1966, this was a major rivalry for both teams, as both were part of the same division. In 1967 they were placed in separate divisions, but remained in the same conference for three years. In 1970 the Steelers (along with the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") and [Baltimore Colts](/wiki/Baltimore_Colts "Baltimore Colts")) moved to the [American Football Conference](/wiki/American_Football_Conference "American Football Conference"), while the Eagles stayed with the rest of the old line NFL teams in the [National Football Conference](/wiki/National_Football_Conference "National Football Conference"). As a result, the Eagles and Steelers no longer played each other every year; instead, they are scheduled to meet once every four years in the regular season. The most recent meeting was in 2024 at Lincoln Financial Field, with the Eagles winning 27–13. The Steelers have lost eleven straight games on the road against the Eagles dating back to 1966, which was also the start of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. As of the 2024 season, the Eagles lead the all-time series 50–29–3.[[262]](#cite_note-263)

### Pro Football Hall of Famers

Main article: [List of Pro Football Hall of Fame inductees](/wiki/List_of_Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame_inductees "List of Pro Football Hall of Fame inductees")

As of 2025, 14 individuals who spent a significant part of their career with the Eagles (shown in **bold** below) have been inducted into the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame").

### Eagles Hall of Fame

See also: [Category:American football museums and halls of fame](/wiki/Category:American_football_museums_and_halls_of_fame "Category:American football museums and halls of fame")

In 1987, the Eagles Honor Roll was established. Every Eagles player who had been elected into the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") at that point was among the inaugural induction class. By 2012, the Honor Roll had been retitled as the Eagles Hall of Fame.[[267]](#cite_note-Honor_roll_2012-268) Players are considered for induction three years after their retirement from the NFL, and there have been 52 inductees into the Eagles Hall of Fame as of 2024.[[268]](#cite_note-269)

From 2008 through 2010, Eagles games were broadcast on both rock-formatted WYSP and sports-talk [Sports Radio 610 WIP](/wiki/WTEL_(AM) "WTEL (AM)"), as both stations were owned and operated by [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") Radio. In 2011, CBS dropped the music on WYSP, renaming it [WIP-FM](/wiki/WIP-FM "WIP-FM") and making it a full simulcast of WIP. By 2020, 610 WIP had changed call letters to WTEL and rebranded to the Philadelphia affiliate for the [Black Information Network](/wiki/Black_Information_Network "Black Information Network"), while WIP-FM broadcast all Eagles games. In 2017, the Eagles extended their broadcasting contract with WIP-FM through 2024.[[271]](#cite_note-272)

[Merrill Reese](/wiki/Merrill_Reese "Merrill Reese"), who joined the Eagles in 1976, is the play-by-play announcer, and former Eagles wide receiver [Mike Quick](/wiki/Mike_Quick "Mike Quick"), who replaced offense lineman [Stan Walters](/wiki/Stan_Walters "Stan Walters") beginning in 1998, is the color analyst.[[272]](#cite_note-e523-273)[[273]](#cite_note-y966-274) The post-game show, which has consisted of many Philadelphia sports personalities, as of the 2014 season is hosted by Kevin Riley, a former Eagles linebacker and special-teamer, and Rob Ellis. Riley was the former post-game host for the show on 94 WYSP before the WIP change over; Rob Ellis hosts a weekly show nightly from 6–10 on 94.1 WIP-FM.

Spanish language broadcasts are on [WEMG](/wiki/WEMG "WEMG") Mega 105.7FM with [Rickie Ricardo](/wiki/Rickie_Ricardo "Rickie Ricardo") on play-by-play, and Oscar Budejen as color commentator.[[274]](#cite_note-z785-275)[[275]](#cite_note-c753-276)

In 2015, the preseason games were being televised on [WCAU](/wiki/WCAU "WCAU"), the local [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") [owned and operated station](/wiki/Owned_and_operated_station "Owned and operated station").

During the regular season, games are governed by the [NFL's master broadcasting contract](/wiki/National_Football_League_on_United_States_television "National Football League on United States television") with [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company"), CBS, NBC, and [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN"). Most games can be seen on Fox-owned [WTXF-TV](/wiki/WTXF-TV "WTXF-TV"). When hosting an AFC team, those games can be seen on CBS-owned [KYW-TV](/wiki/KYW-TV "KYW-TV").

## Training camp

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Eaglestrainingcamp.jpg/250px-Eaglestrainingcamp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eaglestrainingcamp.jpg)

Philadelphia Eagles training camp at the NovaCare Complex in [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia") in 2019

The Eagles previously held their preseason training camp from the end of July through mid-August each year at [Lehigh University](/wiki/Lehigh_University "Lehigh University") in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem,_Pennsylvania "Bethlehem, Pennsylvania") in the [Lehigh Valley](/wiki/Lehigh_Valley "Lehigh Valley").[[276]](#cite_note-277) In 2013, with the addition of head coach [Chip Kelly](/wiki/Chip_Kelly "Chip Kelly"), the Eagles moved their training camp to the [NovaCare](/wiki/NovaCare_Rehabilitation "NovaCare Rehabilitation") Complex (renamed to the [Jefferson Health](/wiki/Jefferson_Health "Jefferson Health") Training Complex in 2026) in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia").[[277]](#cite_note-w836-278)[[278]](#cite_note-TrainingCamp-279)[[279]](#cite_note-280) Training camps were previously held at [Chestnut Hill Academy](/wiki/Chestnut_Hill_Academy "Chestnut Hill Academy") in 1935, [Saint Joseph's University](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_University "Saint Joseph's University") in 1939 and 1943, [Saranac Lake](/wiki/Saranac_Lake,_New_York "Saranac Lake, New York") from 1946 to 1948, [Hershey](/wiki/Hershey,_Pennsylvania "Hershey, Pennsylvania") from 1951 to 1967, [Albright College](/wiki/Albright_College "Albright College") from 1968 to 1972, [Widener University](/wiki/Widener_University "Widener University") from 1973 to 1979, and [West Chester University](/wiki/West_Chester_University "West Chester University") from 1980 to 1995.[[278]](#cite_note-TrainingCamp-279)

## Fight song

Main article: [Fly, Eagles Fly](/wiki/Fly,_Eagles_Fly "Fly, Eagles Fly")

This fight song is heard during Eagles' home games after touchdowns and before the team is introduced prior to kickoff.[[280]](#cite_note-t037-281)

Main article: [Philadelphia Eagles Cheerleaders](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles_Cheerleaders "Philadelphia Eagles Cheerleaders")

[Philadelphia Eagles Cheerleaders](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles_Cheerleaders "Philadelphia Eagles Cheerleaders") in 2008

The Eagles have their own [cheerleading squad](/wiki/National_Football_League_Cheerleading "National Football League Cheerleading"), which performs a variety of dance routines for the fans and the Eagles on the sideline.[[281]](#cite_note-282) The squad also releases a swimsuit calendar each year, and is the first squad in the league to release the calendar on the [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") and [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") mobile systems.[[282]](#cite_note-283)[[283]](#cite_note-284)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/An_Eagles_fan_celebrates_as_confetti_falls_on_the_field_at_Super_Bowl_2018%2C_Minneapolis_MN_%2840074198602%29.jpg/250px-An_Eagles_fan_celebrates_as_confetti_falls_on_the_field_at_Super_Bowl_2018%2C_Minneapolis_MN_%2840074198602%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:An_Eagles_fan_celebrates_as_confetti_falls_on_the_field_at_Super_Bowl_2018,_Minneapolis_MN_(40074198602).jpg)

### Devotion

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/An_Eagles_fan_celebrates_in_the_stands_at_Super_Bowl_2018%2C_Minneapolis_MN_%2825234676207%29.jpg/250px-An_Eagles_fan_celebrates_in_the_stands_at_Super_Bowl_2018%2C_Minneapolis_MN_%2825234676207%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:An_Eagles_fan_celebrates_in_the_stands_at_Super_Bowl_2018,_Minneapolis_MN_(25234676207).jpg)

A Philadelphia Eagles fan celebrating at the [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII") in Minneapolis

Although the method may vary, studies that attempt to rank the 32 fan bases in the [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") consistently place Eagles [fans](/wiki/Sports_fan "Sports fan") among the best in the league, noting their "unmatched fervor".[[284]](#cite_note-forbes080901c-285) [American City Business Journals](/wiki/American_City_Business_Journals "American City Business Journals"), which conducts a regular study to determine the most loyal fans in the NFL,[[285]](#cite_note-bizjournal060904c-286) ranked Eagles fans third in both 1999[[286]](#cite_note-phillybizjournal990205-287) and 2006.[[287]](#cite_note-bizjournal060904a-288) The 2006 study called Eagles fans "incredibly loyal", noting that they filled 99.8% of the seats in the stadium over the previous decade.[[288]](#cite_note-bizjournal060904b-289) *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* placed the Eagles fans first in its 2008 survey,[[289]](#cite_note-forbes080901a-290) which was based on the correlation between team performance and fan attendance.[[290]](#cite_note-forbes080901b-291) [ESPN.com](/wiki/ESPN.com "ESPN.com") placed Eagles fans fourth in the league in its 2008 survey, citing the connection between the team's performance and the mood of the city.[[291]](#cite_note-espn080829-292) The last home game that was blacked out on television in the Philadelphia market as a result of not being sold out was against the Arizona Cardinals on Sunday, September 12, 1999, which was Andy Reid's first home game as new head coach of the Eagles.[[292]](#cite_note-293)

The studies note that, whether the Eagles have winning or losing teams, fans can be counted on to pack the stadium each year. As of August 2008, the team had sold out 71 consecutive games, and 70,000 additional fans were on the Eagles' waiting list for season tickets.[[291]](#cite_note-espn080829-292) Despite finishing with a 6–10 record in the 2005 season, the Eagles ranked second in the NFL in merchandise sales, and single-game tickets for the next season were sold out minutes after phone and Internet lines opened.[[293]](#cite_note-berman-294)

Eagles fans have also been known to chant the famous, "E-A-G-L-E-S – Eagles!" at [Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers"), [Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies"), and [76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers") games when the team is getting [blown out](/wiki/Blowout_(sports) "Blowout (sports)") late in a game and a loss is inevitable, signifying their displeasure with the given team's performance, and that they are instead putting their hope into the Eagles.[[294]](#cite_note-v992-295) Occasionally, travelling Phillies fans may even chant this when the Phillies appear to be a few innings away from an inevitable win on the road as a way to celebrate their team and Philadelphia as a whole. The Eagles and Phillies were both the main tenants at Veterans Stadium before Lincoln Financial Field and [Citizens Bank Park](/wiki/Citizens_Bank_Park "Citizens Bank Park") were built.

Notable famous fans of the Eagles include actors [Bradley Cooper](/wiki/Bradley_Cooper "Bradley Cooper"),[[295]](#cite_note-296) [Rob McElhenney](/wiki/Rob_McElhenney "Rob McElhenney"),[[296]](#cite_note-297) and [Miles Teller](/wiki/Miles_Teller "Miles Teller"),[[297]](#cite_note-298) actor and comedian [Kevin Hart](/wiki/Kevin_Hart "Kevin Hart"),[[298]](#cite_note-z262-299) *[Jeopardy!](/wiki/Jeopardy! "Jeopardy!")* champion [Brad Rutter](/wiki/Brad_Rutter "Brad Rutter"),[[299]](#cite_note-300) journalists [Jake Tapper](/wiki/Jake_Tapper "Jake Tapper")[[300]](#cite_note-301) and [Jesse Watters](/wiki/Jesse_Watters "Jesse Watters"),[[301]](#cite_note-302) morning talk show host [Kelly Ripa](/wiki/Kelly_Ripa "Kelly Ripa"),[[302]](#cite_note-303) TV personality [Jim Cramer](/wiki/Jim_Cramer "Jim Cramer"),[[303]](#cite_note-o652-304) and soccer star [Carli Lloyd](/wiki/Carli_Lloyd "Carli Lloyd"), the latter of whom also appeared at a joint practice of the Eagles with the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") in 2019 where she nailed a 55-yard field goal attempt.[[304]](#cite_note-305) One week later, during an international [friendly](/wiki/Friendly_match "Friendly match") pitting the [United States](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_soccer_team "United States women's national soccer team") against [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_women%27s_national_football_team "Portugal women's national football team") at Lincoln Financial Field, Lloyd celebrated scoring a goal by doing the "E-A-G-L-E-S, Eagles!" chant.[[305]](#cite_note-306) The United States ultimately won that match 4–0.

The Eagles also have famous fans from outside of the United States, which include Australian professional wrestler [Grayson Waller](/wiki/Grayson_Waller "Grayson Waller"),[[306]](#cite_note-307)[[307]](#cite_note-308)[[308]](#cite_note-309) who currently wrestles in American pro wrestling promotion [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE") ([WrestleMania XL](/wiki/WrestleMania_XL "WrestleMania XL"), which was held at Lincoln Financial Field itself, saw Waller don Eagles-themed ring gear[[309]](#cite_note-310)), and [Andrea Bain](/wiki/Andrea_Bain "Andrea Bain"),[[310]](#cite_note-311)[[311]](#cite_note-312) a Canadian talk show host best known for her work as one of the four hosts of the lifestyle talk show *[The Social](/wiki/The_Social_(talk_show) "The Social (talk show)")* on Canadian network [CTV](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network").

### Bad behavior

Along with their [fierce devotion](/wiki/Ultras "Ultras"), Eagles fans have [a reputation for bad behavior](/wiki/Hooliganism "Hooliganism") and [sports-related violence](/wiki/Violence_in_sports "Violence in sports"), especially when the team plays its rivals.[[312]](#cite_note-anderson20021029-313) One of the most famous incidents occurred in the 1968 season when fans of the struggling 2–11 Eagles [pelted snowballs at an incarnation of Santa Claus](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles_Santa_Claus_incident "Philadelphia Eagles Santa Claus incident") during a halftime Christmas celebration.[[313]](#cite_note-314)

In 2006, [HarperCollins](/wiki/HarperCollins "HarperCollins") published *If Football's a Religion, Why Don't We Have a Prayer?*, a book by Jereé Longman, in which he describes the fans of the [700 Level](/wiki/700_Level "700 Level") at [Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium") as having a reputation for "[hostile taunting](/wiki/Crowd_abuse "Crowd abuse"), fighting, public urination and general strangeness."[[314]](#cite_note-315)

A 1997 game against the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") in Philadelphia featured so many incidents that, beginning at the following home game, the Eagles appointed a judge, [Seamus McCaffery](/wiki/Seamus_McCaffery "Seamus McCaffery"), to preside over a temporary courtroom at the stadium; 20 suspects came before him that day.[[312]](#cite_note-anderson20021029-313) Fan behavior improved after the team's move to Lincoln Financial Field, and "Eagles Court" ended in December 2003.[[315]](#cite_note-ap20031206-316)

During a January 2025 home playoff game against the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers"), an Eagles fan shouted obscenities at a woman wearing Packers gear; the incident went viral online, leading to the Eagles fan losing his job and receiving a ban from the stadium.[[316]](#cite_note-317)

## In popular culture

The [1976 Philadelphia Eagles season](/wiki/1976_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1976 Philadelphia Eagles season") was the subject of the movie *[Invincible](/wiki/Invincible_(2006_film) "Invincible (2006 film)")*.[[317]](#cite_note-t713-318) The movie stars [Mark Wahlberg](/wiki/Mark_Wahlberg "Mark Wahlberg") as [Vince Papale](/wiki/Vince_Papale "Vince Papale"), a part-time school teacher and diehard Eagles fan who became an Eagles player. The film differs slightly from true events as the selection process was invitation-only, and Papale had at least some previous playing experience.[[318]](#cite_note-319) The film *[Silver Linings Playbook](/wiki/Silver_Linings_Playbook "Silver Linings Playbook")* highlights the [2008 Philadelphia Eagles season](/wiki/2008_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2008 Philadelphia Eagles season"), and the novel mentions the [2006 team](/wiki/2006_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2006 Philadelphia Eagles season").[[319]](#cite_note-h265-320)

The comedy television series *[It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia](/wiki/It%27s_Always_Sunny_in_Philadelphia "It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia")* makes several references to the Philadelphia Eagles, most notably on Season 3, Episode 2, "The Gang Gets Invincible", a reference to the 2006 Wahlberg film.[[320]](#cite_note-321)
